The **X-Men** are a [superhero](/wiki/Superhero "Superhero") team in [American comic books](/wiki/American_comic_book "American comic book") published by [Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics"). Created by writer/editor [Stan Lee](/wiki/Stan_Lee "Stan Lee") and artist/co-plotter [Jack Kirby](/wiki/Jack_Kirby "Jack Kirby"), the team first appeared in [*The X-Men*](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men") #1 (September 1963).[[1]](#cite_note-1) Although initially cancelled in 1970 due to low sales, following its [1975 revival](/wiki/Giant-Size_X-Men "Giant-Size X-Men") and subsequent direction under writer [Chris Claremont](/wiki/Chris_Claremont "Chris Claremont"), it became one of Marvel Comics's most recognizable and successful franchises.[[2]](#cite_note-2) They have appeared in numerous books, [television shows](/wiki/X-Men_in_television "X-Men in television"), [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Studios "20th Century Studios")'s [*X-Men* films](/wiki/X-Men_(film_series) "X-Men (film series)"), and [video games](/wiki/List_of_video_games_featuring_the_X-Men "List of video games featuring the X-Men"). The *X-Men* title may refer to the superhero team itself, [the eponymous comic series](/wiki/X-Men_(comic_book) "X-Men (comic book)"), or the broader franchise, which includes [various solo titles and team books](/wiki/List_of_X-Men_comics "List of X-Men comics"), such as the [New Mutants](/wiki/New_Mutants "New Mutants"), [Excalibur](/wiki/Excalibur_(comics) "Excalibur (comics)"), and [X-Force](/wiki/X-Force "X-Force").

In the [Marvel Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Universe "Marvel Universe"), [mutants](/wiki/Mutant_(Marvel_Comics) "Mutant (Marvel Comics)") are humans born with a genetic trait called the X-gene, which grants them natural superhuman abilities, generally manifesting during [puberty](/wiki/Puberty "Puberty"). Due to their differences from most humanity, mutants are subject to prejudice and discrimination; many X-Men stories feature social commentary on bigotry, justice, and other political themes. The X-Men have fought against various [enemies](/wiki/List_of_X-Men_enemies "List of X-Men enemies"), including villainous mutants, human bigots, supervillains, mystical threats, extraterrestrials, and evil artificial intelligences. In most iterations of the team, they are led by their founder, [Charles Xavier / Professor X](/wiki/Professor_X "Professor X"), a powerful telepath who runs a school for mutant children out of [his mansion in Westchester, New York](/wiki/X-Mansion "X-Mansion"), which secretly is also the headquarters of the X-Men. Their stories have frequently involved [Magneto](/wiki/Magneto_(Marvel_Comics) "Magneto (Marvel Comics)"), a powerful mutant with control over [magnetic fields](/wiki/Magnetic_field "Magnetic field"), who is depicted as an old friend of and [foil](/wiki/Foil_(narrative) "Foil (narrative)") to Xavier, acting as an adversary or ally.

## Background and creation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background and creation")]

In 1963, with the success of the [Fantastic Four](/wiki/Fantastic_Four "Fantastic Four"), co-creator [Stan Lee](/wiki/Stan_Lee "Stan Lee") wanted to create another group of superheroes. Unlike Lee's earlier creations such as [Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man "Spider-Man") who acquired their powers through scientific means, Lee decided that this new group of heroes were "[mutants](/wiki/Mutant_(Marvel_Comics) "Mutant (Marvel Comics)")", born with powers as he had grown weary of creating separate origins for each superhero.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023xxv-4)

In a 1987 interview, Kirby said:

> The X-Men, I did the natural thing there. What would you do with mutants who were just plain boys and girls and certainly not dangerous? You school them. You develop their skills. So I gave them a teacher, Professor X. Of course, it was the natural thing to do, instead of disorienting or alienating people who were different from us, I made the X-Men part of the human race, which they were. Possibly, radiation, if it is beneficial, may create mutants that'll save us instead of doing us harm. I felt that if we train the mutants our way, they'll help us – and not only help us, but achieve a measure of growth in their own sense. And so, we could all live together.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Lee devised the series title after Marvel publisher [Martin Goodman](/wiki/Martin_Goodman_(publisher) "Martin Goodman (publisher)") turned down the initial name, "The Mutants," stating that readers would not know what a "mutant" was.[[6]](#cite_note-sonoforigins-6)

Within the [Marvel Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Universe "Marvel Universe"), the X-Men are widely regarded to have been named after [Professor X](/wiki/Professor_X "Professor X"). The original explanation for the name, as provided by Xavier in *The X-Men* #1 (1963), is that mutants "possess an extra power ... one which ordinary humans do not!! That is why I call my students ... X-Men, for EX-tra power!"[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Publication history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Publication history")]

Further information: [List of X-Men comics](/wiki/List_of_X-Men_comics "List of X-Men comics")

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1960s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Original_X-Men.png/250px-Original_X-Men.png)](/wiki/File:Original_X-Men.png)

The original X-Men members who were created by [Stan Lee](/wiki/Stan_Lee "Stan Lee") and [Jack Kirby](/wiki/Jack_Kirby "Jack Kirby"), showing their original design

The first issue, [cover-dated](/wiki/Cover-date "Cover-date") September 1963,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders20232-8) introduces the original team, with [Marvel Girl](/wiki/Jean_Grey "Jean Grey") presented as a new pupil at Charles Xavier's school, apparently the first female student, and meeting [Cyclops](/wiki/Cyclops_(Marvel_Comics) "Cyclops (Marvel Comics)"), [Beast](/wiki/Beast_(Marvel_Comics) "Beast (Marvel Comics)"), [Angel](/wiki/Warren_Worthington_III "Warren Worthington III"), and [Iceman](/wiki/Iceman_(Marvel_Comics) "Iceman (Marvel Comics)").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023xxix-9) Cyclops, whose eyes shoot powerful beams unless they are controlled by a visor, is the central protagonist.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021138–139-10) Beast is apelike in appearance but speaks in sophisticated diction; Iceman is hedonistic and energetic; Angel comes from a wealthy background and has wings protruding from his back; and Marvel Girl is hyperfeminine. The masculine characters often express their attraction to Marvel Girl.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021139-11) The first issue also introduces the team's archenemy, [Magneto](/wiki/Magneto_(Marvel_Comics) "Magneto (Marvel Comics)").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023xxx–xxxi-12) Magneto appears frequently throughout the series.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023229-13)

Early *The X-Men* issues (#4 and #5) go on to introduce Magneto's [Brotherhood of Evil Mutants](/wiki/Brotherhood_of_Mutants "Brotherhood of Mutants"), featuring [Mastermind](/wiki/Mastermind_(Jason_Wyngarde) "Mastermind (Jason Wyngarde)") and [Toad](/wiki/Toad_(Marvel_Comics) "Toad (Marvel Comics)").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023xxxii-14) [Quicksilver](/wiki/Quicksilver_(Marvel_Comics) "Quicksilver (Marvel Comics)") and [Scarlet Witch](/wiki/Scarlet_Witch "Scarlet Witch") were initially introduced in issue #4 as members of the Brotherhood, but later reformed and joined the [Avengers](/wiki/Avengers_(comics) "Avengers (comics)").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco202293-15) The comic focused on a common human theme of good versus evil; issue #8 first introduces the theme of [prejudice](/wiki/Prejudice "Prejudice") against mutants,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023xxxiii–xxxiv-16) which eventually comes to function as an allegory for [racism](/wiki/Racism "Racism") or other forms of bigotry.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021138-17) Issue #12 introduces Cain Marko, the [Juggernaut](/wiki/Juggernaut_(character) "Juggernaut (character)"), Professor Xavier's stepbrother, and implies that Marko was responsible for the accident that paralyzed Xavier.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023160-18) Issues #14 to #16 (November 1965 - January 1966) introduce the [Sentinels](/wiki/Sentinel_(comics) "Sentinel (comics)"), mutant-hunting robots designed by [Bolivar Trask](/wiki/Bolivar_Trask "Bolivar Trask").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023xxxiv-19) The title lagged in sales behind Marvel's other comic franchises. Writer [Roy Thomas](/wiki/Roy_Thomas "Roy Thomas") and [Werner Roth](/wiki/Werner_Roth_(comics) "Werner Roth (comics)") replaced Lee and Kirby by issue #20 (May 1966).[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023xxxv-20) Issue #28 introduces [Banshee](/wiki/Banshee_(character) "Banshee (character)"); initially an antagonist, Banshee will go on to become a member of the X-Men.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023230-21)

In 1969, Thomas and illustrator [Neal Adams](/wiki/Neal_Adams "Neal Adams") briefly rejuvenated the comic book.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023xxxix-22) They gave regular roles to two recently introduced characters: Alex Summers (Cyclops' brother, later known as [Havok](/wiki/Havok_(character) "Havok (character)"), who had been introduced by Roy Thomas before Adams began work on the comic) and Lorna Dane, later called [Polaris](/wiki/Polaris_(Marvel_Comics) "Polaris (Marvel Comics)") (created by [Arnold Drake](/wiki/Arnold_Drake "Arnold Drake") and [Jim Steranko](/wiki/Jim_Steranko "Jim Steranko")).[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023290–291-23) Issue #64 introduced [Sunfire](/wiki/Sunfire_(character) "Sunfire (character)"), a Japanese mutant. However, these later *X-Men* issues failed to attract sales and Marvel stopped producing new stories with issue #66 (March 1970), later reprinting a number of the older comics as issues #67–93.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2023362-24)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/61/Giant-Size_X-Men_%28no._1_-_cover%29.jpg/250px-Giant-Size_X-Men_%28no._1_-_cover%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giant-Size_X-Men_(no._1_-_cover).jpg)

[Giant-Size X-Men](/wiki/Giant-Size_X-Men "Giant-Size X-Men") #1 (May 1975). Cover art by Gil Kane and Dave Cockrum.

In *[Giant-Size X-Men](/wiki/Giant-Size_X-Men "Giant-Size X-Men")* #1 (1975), writer [Len Wein](/wiki/Len_Wein "Len Wein") and artist [Dave Cockrum](/wiki/Dave_Cockrum "Dave Cockrum") introduced a new team that starred in a revival of *The X-Men*. The new team featured in a reboot of the ongoing *X-Men* series, which [Chris Claremont](/wiki/Chris_Claremont "Chris Claremont") began writing with [issue #94](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men_94 "Uncanny X-Men 94"), with Cockrum continuing as illustrator.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021139–140-25) This new team replaced the previous members with the exception of [Cyclops](/wiki/Cyclops_(Marvel_Comics) "Cyclops (Marvel Comics)"), who remained. This team differed greatly from the original, now "an international cadre of mutants with diverse and often traumatic personal histories".[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016145-26) Unlike in the early issues of the original series, the new team was not made up of teenagers. Marvel's corporate owners, [Cadence Industries](/wiki/Cadence_Industries "Cadence Industries"), had suggested the new team should be international, feeling it needed characters with "foreign appeal".[[27]](#cite_note-27) So each character was from a different country with varying cultural and philosophical beliefs, and all were already well-versed in using their mutant powers, several being experienced in combat.

The "all-new, all-different X-Men"[[28]](#cite_note-28) were led by Cyclops, from the original team,[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeman202343-29) and consisted of the newly created [Colossus](/wiki/Colossus_(character) "Colossus (character)") (from the Soviet Union/Russia), [Nightcrawler](/wiki/Nightcrawler_(character) "Nightcrawler (character)") (from Germany), [Storm](/wiki/Storm_(Marvel_Comics) "Storm (Marvel Comics)") (from Kenya), and [Thunderbird](/wiki/Thunderbird_(John_Proudstar) "Thunderbird (John Proudstar)") (a Native American of [Apache](/wiki/Apache "Apache") descent), and three previously introduced characters: Banshee (from Ireland), Sunfire (from Japan), and [Wolverine](/wiki/Wolverine_(character) "Wolverine (character)") (from Canada).[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016145-26) Wolverine had been previously introduced as an antagonist of the [Hulk](/wiki/Hulk "Hulk").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill20259-30) However, this team would not remain whole for long; Sunfire quit shortly after their first mission, and Thunderbird died on the next.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeman202377-31) Filling in the vacancy, a revamped [Jean Grey](/wiki/Jean_Grey "Jean Grey") soon rejoined the X-Men. Claremont emphasized the character development of Grey, whose powers increased dramatically as she adopted a new name, Phoenix in issue #101 (October 1976).[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021140–141-32) Angel, Beast, Iceman, Havok, and Polaris also made significant guest appearances.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Claremont introduced a significant non-mutant supporting character, [Moira MacTaggert](/wiki/Moira_MacTaggert "Moira MacTaggert"), in issue #96; MacTaggert operates a genetic research facility on [Muir Island](/wiki/Muir_Island "Muir Island"). Claremont became known for his strong female characters, sometimes called "Claremont Women."[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeman202322-33) The series presents Storm and Phoenix as close friends, in a manner that scholar Ramzi Fawaz views as allegorical for alliances between liberal feminists and emerging [Black feminism](/wiki/Black_feminism "Black feminism").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016149–155-34) 1977 presented a new [space opera](/wiki/Space_opera "Space opera") story line, "The Phoenix Saga", that particularly emphasizes the alliance of these two characters; Fawaz calls it "arguably the most canonical story line in *The X-Men'*s publishing history."[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016155-35) The story includes the [Shi'ar Empire](/wiki/Shi%27ar_Empire "Shi'ar Empire"), [Lilandra Neramani](/wiki/Lilandra_Neramani "Lilandra Neramani"), and the [Starjammers](/wiki/Starjammers "Starjammers").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016155–156-36)

Beginning with issue #108 (December 1977), [John Byrne](/wiki/John_Byrne_(comics) "John Byrne (comics)") replaced Cockrum as the primary artist.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanderson2022171-37) Claremont and Byrne had previously collaborated on the *[Iron Fist](/wiki/Iron_Fist_(character) "Iron Fist (character)")* and *[Luke Cage](/wiki/Luke_Cage "Luke Cage")* series.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021141-38) Their run met with critical acclaim,[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021142-39) and produced such landmark storylines as the introduction of [Alpha Flight](/wiki/Alpha_Flight "Alpha Flight") and the [Proteus](/wiki/Proteus_(Marvel_Comics) "Proteus (Marvel Comics)") saga.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Other characters introduced during this time include [Amanda Sefton](/wiki/Amanda_Sefton "Amanda Sefton") and [Mystique](/wiki/Mystique_(character) "Mystique (character)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Wolverine is initially overshadowed by the other characters, although he creates tension in the team as he is attracted to Jean Grey, who is Cyclops' girlfriend.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202527-41) While Claremont considered writing Wolverine out of the series, Byrne advocated for his continuing inclusion, partly because the character is Canadian and this is also Byrne's nationality. Wolverine became increasingly developed and popular as a character, becoming the [breakout character](/wiki/Breakout_character "Breakout character") on the team.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021151–152-42) *X-Men* #100 introduces the "fastball special", a combat maneuver in which the super-strong [Colossus](/wiki/Colossus_(character) "Colossus (character)") throws Wolverine at a distance as if he were a projectile weapon; this tactic recurs in many future battles.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202516-43) Stories of the late 1970s establish Wolverine's murky past and unstable nature, which he battles to keep in check.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021151–152-42) Wolverine's name was revealed as "Logan" in *X-Men* #103 (February 1977);[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202517-44) *X-Men* #116 (December 1978) first introduces Wolverine's healing factor, and issue #124 (August 1979) first suggests that he has a reinforced skeleton.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202523-45)

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1980s")]

The 1980s began with the comic's best-known story arc, the "[Dark Phoenix Saga](/wiki/The_Dark_Phoenix_Saga "The Dark Phoenix Saga")", which saw Phoenix manipulated by Mastermind, now a member of the [Hellfire Club](/wiki/Hellfire_Club_(comics) "Hellfire Club (comics)"),[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016214–215-46) and becoming corrupted with an overwhelming lust for power and destruction as the evil Dark Phoenix.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021152-47) The Hellfire Club also included the telepathic *[femme fatale](/wiki/Femme_fatale "Femme fatale")* [Emma Frost](/wiki/Emma_Frost "Emma Frost"), who became an increasingly significant character.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022186-48) The story results in the death of Jean Grey.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021153-49) The [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") singer mutant, [Dazzler](/wiki/Dazzler_(Marvel_Comics) "Dazzler (Marvel Comics)"), previously introduced in her own miniseries, also appeared in the story and eventually joined the X-Men.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022186–87-50) This story arc also introduces a new character, the exuberant young girl [Kitty Pryde](/wiki/Kitty_Pryde "Kitty Pryde").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021154-51) Thirteen years old, Pryde is Marvel's first canonically Jewish superhero.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeman202353-52) Immediately after, Claremont and Byrne created "[Days of Future Past](/wiki/Days_of_Future_Past "Days of Future Past")", in which Wolverine and Kitty Pryde experience a dystopian future for mutants.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021154–156-53) Byrne left the series shortly after its completion.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021156-54) The [Morlocks](/wiki/Morlocks_(comics) "Morlocks (comics)"), deformed mutants who live underground, first appear in *The Uncanny X-Men* #169 (May 1983).[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022218-55) In 1983, Cyclops meets [Madelyne Pryor](/wiki/Madelyne_Pryor "Madelyne Pryor"), eventually revealed as a clone of Jean Grey.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022200-56) Other important storylines include the saga of [Deathbird](/wiki/Deathbird "Deathbird") and the [Brood](/wiki/Brood_(comics) "Brood (comics)"), the invasion of the Dire Wraiths and *The Trial of Magneto!*, as well as *[X-Men: God Loves, Man Kills](/wiki/X-Men:_God_Loves,_Man_Kills "X-Men: God Loves, Man Kills"),* the partial inspiration for [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox")'s film *[X2: X-Men United](/wiki/X2_(film) "X2 (film)")* (2003).[[57]](#cite_note-next-57) In this period, Claremont and Byrne particularly emphasized the theme that mutants are oppressed by human bigotry.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021147-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a2/The_cover_of_1987%27s_Uncanny_X-Men_227.jpg/250px-The_cover_of_1987%27s_Uncanny_X-Men_227.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_cover_of_1987%27s_Uncanny_X-Men_227.jpg)

*Uncanny X-Men* #227 (March 1988) by Chris Claremont and Marc Silvestri

By the early 1980s, *X-Men* was Marvel's top-selling comic title. Its sales were such that distributors and retailers began using an "X-Men index", rating each comic book publication by how many orders it garnered compared to that month's issue of *X-Men*.[[59]](#cite_note-RightChord-59) The growing popularity of *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men")* led to the introduction of a number of ongoing spin-off series, such as the ongoing *[New Mutants](/wiki/New_Mutants "New Mutants")*, about a younger adjunct team, which began in 1982. As Fawaz puts it, "Unlike the X-Men, the New Mutants were brought together not for the purposes of an egalitarian peace-keeping mission but to learn the proper use of their powers, which they experienced as monstrous physical burdens threatening their own safety and that of their loved ones."[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016234-60) He describes them as presenting the "most diverse cast of any mainstream superhero comic," including [Karma](/wiki/Karma_(character) "Karma (character)") (Vietnamese), [Danielle Moonstar](/wiki/Danielle_Moonstar "Danielle Moonstar") (Cheyenne), [Sunspot](/wiki/Sunspot_(Marvel_Comics) "Sunspot (Marvel Comics)") (Brazil), [Cannonball](/wiki/Cannonball_(Marvel_Comics) "Cannonball (Marvel Comics)") (southern U.S.), and [Wolfsbane](/wiki/Wolfsbane_(character) "Wolfsbane (character)") (Scotland), and [Magik](/wiki/Magik "Magik") (Russia).[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016238–248-61) In 1984, Claremont collaborated with [Bill Sienkiewicz](/wiki/Bill_Sienkiewicz "Bill Sienkiewicz") for the New Mutants' "Demon Bear Saga", which was critically acclaimed.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016256–264-62)

Spin-off miniseries included the first four-issue *[Wolverine](/wiki/Wolverine_(comic_book) "Wolverine (comic book)")* solo miniseries (September–December 1982), by Claremont and [Frank Miller](/wiki/Frank_Miller "Frank Miller"),[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202530-63) as well as the following six-issue *[Kitty Pryde and Wolverine](/wiki/Kitty_Pryde_and_Wolverine "Kitty Pryde and Wolverine")* by Claremont and [Al Milgrom](/wiki/Al_Milgrom "Al Milgrom") (Nov. 1984 – April 1985).[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202532-64) Other miniseries included *Storm and Illyana: Magik* (1983) and *X-Men and the Micronauts*.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021160-65) In 1983, [Alpha Flight](/wiki/Alpha_Flight "Alpha Flight"), a group of Canadian superheroes created by Byrne as Wolverine's original team, received their own ongoing series.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanderson2022179-66)

Comics scholar Douglas Wolk describes autumn 1985 as the "peak of *X-Men'*s world beating phase", when a single month produced the double-sized *Uncanny X-Men* #200, the two-part miniseries *X-Men/Alpha Flight*, an X-Men Annual, a New Mutants Special Edition, and Heroes for Hope, a fundraiser for relief of the [1983-1985 famine in Ethiopia](/wiki/1983-1985_famine_in_Ethiopia "1983-1985 famine in Ethiopia"). The following year, a new series began, resurrecting Jean Grey and re-uniting the original X-Men under the name *[X-Factor](/wiki/X-Factor_(comics) "X-Factor (comics)")*. The series was initially written by [Bob Layton](/wiki/Bob_Layton "Bob Layton"), without Claremont's participation.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021162-67)

In a controversial move, [Professor X](/wiki/Professor_X "Professor X") relocated to outer space to be with [Lilandra Neramani](/wiki/Lilandra_Neramani "Lilandra Neramani"), Majestrix of the [Shi'ar](/wiki/Shi%27ar "Shi'ar") empire, in 1986. Magneto then joined the X-Men in Xavier's place and became the director of the New Mutants.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield202575-68) The Darwinian villain [Apocalypse](/wiki/Apocalypse_(character) "Apocalypse (character)") first appeared in *X-Factor* #6 (July 1986).[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022217-69) [Longshot](/wiki/Longshot_(Marvel_Comics) "Longshot (Marvel Comics)") who had been first introduced in his own miniseries the previous year, joined the X-Men in Annual #10 (1986).[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022212-70) Longshot came from another dimension called the Mojoverse, ruled by a mad dictator obsessed with TV named [Mojo](/wiki/Mojo_(comics) "Mojo (comics)"), who also became a recurrent adversary of the X-Men.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022213-71)

When Claremont conceived a story arc in 1986, the "[Mutant Massacre](/wiki/Mutant_Massacre "Mutant Massacre")", which was too long to run in the monthly *X-Men*, editor [Louise Simonson](/wiki/Louise_Simonson "Louise Simonson") decided to have it overlap into several X-Books. In the story, many of the Morlocks are slaughtered by new villains, the [Marauders](/wiki/Marauders_(comics) "Marauders (comics)").[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022218-55) The story line brought [Sabretooth](/wiki/Sabretooth_(character) "Sabretooth (character)") into conflict with the X-Men and particularly Wolverine.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202538–40-72) The story was a major financial success.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In 1987, Claremont wrote a new crossover, "[The Fall of the Mutants](/wiki/The_Fall_of_the_Mutants "The Fall of the Mutants")", in which the team appears to have been killed but actually relocates to Australia. In the course of the story, Angel has his wings removed; Apocalyapse turns him into a cyborg and replaces them with new mechanical wings, and he takes on the name "Archangel."[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022224-74) Over the next years, the main title had multiple disconnected protagonists. Sales began to decline.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021164-75) The success of the [crossovers](/wiki/Fictional_crossover "Fictional crossover") *Mutant Massacre* and *The Fall of Mutants* in countering these flagging sales led the editors to decide internally that multi-title crossover stories would appear annually.[[76]](#cite_note-futureNow-76)

[Mister Sinister](/wiki/Mister_Sinister "Mister Sinister"), another villain fixated on Darwinism like Apocalypse, first appeared in *The Uncanny X-Men* #221 (1987). Marvel launched an ongoing *Wolverine* title written by Claremont with art by [John Buscema](/wiki/John_Buscema "John Buscema") in November 1988.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202541–47-77) *Uncanny X-Men* #235 (October 1988) introduces the oppressive nation of [Genosha](/wiki/Genosha "Genosha"), an allegory for [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022226-78) 1989 saw the publication of the [Inferno](/wiki/Inferno_(Marvel_Comics) "Inferno (Marvel Comics)") crossover, in which the X-Men struggle to rescue Magik from demonic forces.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFawaz2016264–266-79) *The Uncanny X-Men* #244 (May 1989) introduces [Jubilee](/wiki/Jubilee_(character) "Jubilee (character)"), a Chinese American teenager, to the X-Men team. [Marc Silvestri](/wiki/Marc_Silvestri "Marc Silvestri") became one of the most prominent artists for the series in the late 1980s.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFalco2022229-80)

In the Claremont era, [Storm](/wiki/Storm_(Marvel_Comics) "Storm (Marvel Comics)") is the most prominent character and the main protagonist.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeman202335-81) The shapeshifter [Mystique](/wiki/Mystique_(character) "Mystique (character)") is the most commonly appearing villain of the period.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021149-82)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeman202319-83) Other new additions to the X-Men during this time were [Rogue](/wiki/Rogue_(Marvel_Comics) "Rogue (Marvel Comics)"),[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeman202354-84) [Psylocke](/wiki/Betsy_Braddock "Betsy Braddock"),[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeman202355-85) and [Forge](/wiki/Forge_(character) "Forge (character)").[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECebulski2024159-86)

* X-Men
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men"),* vol. 1 (flagship) – a team of young mutants with superhuman abilities led and taught by Professor X (1963–1970); the team expanded when Xavier recruited mutants from around the world (1975–1985); a reformed Magneto became the headmaster after Xavier had left Earth (1985–1988); the team later relocated to the Australian Outback after the events of *The* *Fall of the Mutants* (1988–1989); after the X-Men is disassembled, the team reformed to fight the mutant-rights abuse of Genosha (1991).
  + *[X-Factor](/wiki/X-Factor_(comic_book) "X-Factor (comic book)"),* vol. 1 – the Original Five set up a business advertised as mutant-hunters for hire, and secretly trained the captured mutants to control their powers and reintegrate them into society (1986–1991).[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025106–108-87)
  + [*Excalibur*](/wiki/Excalibur_(comic_book) "Excalibur (comic book)"), vol. 1 – Nightcrawler, Shadowcat and Rachel Grey teamed up with Captain Britain and Meggan to form a group of mutants based in Europe after the apparent death of the X-Men during *The Fall of the Mutants* (1988–1992).[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025109–111-88)
* X-Men in Training
  + *[New Mutants](/wiki/The_New_Mutants_(comic_book) "The New Mutants (comic book)"),* vol. 1 – a group of teenaged students of the School for Gifted Youngsters gathered by Professor X.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025100–102-89)
* Other Teams
  + *[Alpha Flight](/wiki/Alpha_Flight_(comic_book) "Alpha Flight (comic book)"),* vol. 1 – Canada's premiere team of super-heroes organized under the auspices of the Canadian government's Department H.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025106-90)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1990s")]

[Gambit](/wiki/Gambit_(Marvel_Comics) "Gambit (Marvel Comics)") first appeared *Uncanny X-Men* #266 (Aug. 1990) and became one of the most popular X-Men of the decade, also pursuing a romance with Rogue.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022236-91) The "[X-Tinction Agenda](/wiki/X-Men:_X-Tinction_Agenda "X-Men: X-Tinction Agenda")" crossover took place in 1990, featuring artists [Jim Lee](/wiki/Jim_Lee "Jim Lee"), [Rob Liefeld](/wiki/Rob_Liefeld "Rob Liefeld"), and [Jon Bogdanove](/wiki/Jon_Bogdanove "Jon Bogdanove").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022239-92) In August 1991, the *New Mutants* title was cancelled and replaced by *[X-Force](/wiki/X-Force "X-Force")*, a best-selling title written and illustrated by Liefeld. In this series, the New Mutants join a more militant team led by a mysterious cyborg, [Cable](/wiki/Cable_(character) "Cable (character)").[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022242-93)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/12.5.19JimLeeXMenV2HCByLuigiNovi2.jpg/250px-12.5.19JimLeeXMenV2HCByLuigiNovi2.jpg)](/wiki/File:12.5.19JimLeeXMenV2HCByLuigiNovi2.jpg)

Artist [Jim Lee](/wiki/Jim_Lee "Jim Lee") signing a hardcover collected edition of his work on *X-Men* (Vol 2) at [Midtown Comics](/wiki/Midtown_Comics "Midtown Comics") in Manhattan

In October 1991, Marvel revised the entire lineup of X-Men comic book titles, centered on the launch of a second X-Men series, simply titled *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men:_Legacy "X-Men: Legacy")*. Initially written by Claremont and illustrated by Jim Lee, the first issue of this series is the highest selling comic book issue in history, selling more than eight million copies.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichauWiacek2025114-94) Retailers pre-ordered over 8.1 million copies of issue #1, generating and selling nearly $7 million (though retailers probably sold closer to 3 million copies),[[95]](#cite_note-95) making it, according to [Guinness Book of World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records"), the best-selling comic book of all time. Guinness presented honors to Claremont at the 2010 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con").[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)

With the return of Xavier and the original X-Men to the team, the roster was split into two strike forces: Cyclops's "Blue Team" (chronicled in *X-Men*) and Storm's "Gold Team" (in *The Uncanny X-Men*). A new character, [Bishop](/wiki/Bishop_(Marvel_Comics) "Bishop (Marvel Comics)"), a soldier from another dystopian future, soon joined the Gold Team.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022243-99)

Internal friction soon split the X-books' creative teams. In a controversial move, X-Men editor [Bob Harras](/wiki/Bob_Harras "Bob Harras") sided with Lee (and *Uncanny X-Men* artist [Whilce Portacio](/wiki/Whilce_Portacio "Whilce Portacio")) over Claremont in a dispute over plotting.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Claremont left after only three issues of *X-Men*, ending his 16-year run as *X-Men* writer.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021164-75) At the time of his departure, he was the longest-running author of the series.[[101]](#cite_note-101) Marvel replaced Claremont briefly with [John Byrne](/wiki/John_Byrne_(comics) "John Byrne (comics)"), who scripted both books for a few issues. Byrne was then replaced by [Fabian Nicieza](/wiki/Fabian_Nicieza "Fabian Nicieza") and [Scott Lobdell](/wiki/Scott_Lobdell "Scott Lobdell"), who would take over the majority of writing duties for the X-Men until Lee's own departure months later when he and several other popular artists (including former X-title artists Liefeld, Portacio, and [Marc Silvestri](/wiki/Marc_Silvestri "Marc Silvestri")) would leave Marvel to form [Image Comics](/wiki/Image_Comics "Image Comics"). Jim Lee's X-Men designs would be the basis for much of the *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men:_The_Animated_Series "X-Men: The Animated Series")* animated series and action figure line as well as several Capcom video games.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Lobdell and Nicieza, along with [Peter David](/wiki/Peter_David "Peter David"), wrote a new crossover series with artists such as [Brandon Peterson](/wiki/Brandon_Peterson "Brandon Peterson"), [Jae Lee](/wiki/Jae_Lee "Jae Lee"), [Andy Kubert](/wiki/Andy_Kubert "Andy Kubert"), and [Greg Capullo](/wiki/Greg_Capullo "Greg Capullo"), centered on battles with Cable's clone, [Stryfe](/wiki/Stryfe "Stryfe"). The story revealed that Cable is actually the son of Cyclops and Madelyne Pryor, who grew to adulthood in an alternate future and returned to the present by means of time travel.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022247-102) The "[Fatal Attractions](/wiki/Fatal_Attractions_(comics) "Fatal Attractions (comics)")" crossover in late 1993 concerned the return of Magneto, who ripped out Wolverine's [adamantium](/wiki/Adamantium "Adamantium") skeleton, leading to his taking a leave of absence from the team.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowsill202566-103) Gambit also appeared in his own miniseries, beginning in November 1993.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022251-104) Cyclops and Jean Grey got married in *X-Men* #30 (March 1994).[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022255-105) In November 1994, a next generation of students began with *[Generation X](/wiki/Generation_X_(comics) "Generation X (comics)")*, featuring Jubilee and other teenage mutants led and schooled by [Banshee](/wiki/Banshee_(character) "Banshee (character)") and ex-villainess [Emma Frost](/wiki/Emma_Frost "Emma Frost") at her [Massachusetts Academy](/wiki/Massachusetts_Academy_(comics) "Massachusetts Academy (comics)").[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022256-106) Rogue also starred in her own miniseries, beginning in January 1995.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022258-107)

1995 saw "[Age of Apocalypse](/wiki/Age_of_Apocalypse "Age of Apocalypse")", a massive crossover in which all the X-Men related titles were temporarily cancelled and replaced by new miniseries taking place in a dystopian future, recalling the earlier *Days of Future Past* series.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021166–167-108) This was followed by "[Onslaught](/wiki/Onslaught_(Marvel_Comics) "Onslaught (Marvel Comics)")" in 1996,[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022263-109) "[Operation: Zero Tolerance](/wiki/Operation:_Zero_Tolerance "Operation: Zero Tolerance")" in 1997,[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022267-110) "[The Hunt for Xavier](/wiki/The_Hunt_for_Xavier "The Hunt for Xavier")" in 1998, "The Magneto War" in 1999, "[Apocalypse: The Twelve](/wiki/Apocalypse:_The_Twelve "Apocalypse: The Twelve")" / "[Ages of Apocalypse](/wiki/Ages_of_Apocalypse "Ages of Apocalypse")" in 2000 and "[Eve of Destruction](/wiki/X-Men:_Eve_of_Destruction "X-Men: Eve of Destruction")" in 2001. Though the frequent crossovers were criticized by fans as well as editorial and creative staff for being artificially regular, disruptive to the direction of the individual series, and having far less lasting impact than promised, they continued to be financially successful.[[76]](#cite_note-futureNow-76)

* X-Men
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men"),* vol. 1 (flagship) – initially featured the Gold Team strike force led by Storm (1991–1995); later featured a team of X-Men recruited by Gladiator to defend the Shi'ar empire against the Phalanx (1997); the Gold and Blue strike force merged to face new threats including Onslaught, Dark Beast, Shadow King and Magneto (1997–2000); later featured a squad led by Gambit during the *Revolution* revamp (2000–2001). The title is replaced by *Astonishing X-Men* during the *Age of Apocalypse* event.
  + *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_vol._2 "X-Men vol. 2"),* vol. 2 – initially featured the Blue Team strike force led by Cyclops (1991–1995); later featured a new core group consisting of Cannonball, Cyclops, Jean Grey, Storm and Wolverine took on [Sebastian Shaw](/wiki/Sebastian_Shaw_(character) "Sebastian Shaw (character)") and [Bastion](/wiki/Bastion_(comics) "Bastion (comics)") during the events of Operation: Zero Tolerance (1997); members of the *Excalibur* team joined the combined Gold and Blue strike force (1997–2000); later featured a squad led by Rogue during the *Revolution* revamp (2000–2001). The title is replaced by *Amazing X-Men* during the *Age of Apocalypse* event.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025113–115-111)
  + [*X-Force*](/wiki/X-Force_(comic_book) "X-Force (comic book)"), vol. 1 – Cable re-organized the New Mutants into the para-military mutant strike team (1991–1995); the team move in with the X-Men at the X-Mansion and effectively become the X-Men's junior team (1995–1997); the team later move to San Francisco to set up a new headquarter (1997–2001); the team becomes a covert ops superhero team under the leadership of Pete Wisdom during the *Revolution* revamp (2001). The title is replaced by *Gambit & the X-Ternals* during the *Age of Apocalypse* event.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025111–112-112)
* X-Men in Training
  + *[Generation X](/wiki/Generation_X_(comic_book) "Generation X (comic book)"),* vol. 1 – students at the Massachusetts Academy mentored by Banshee and the former villain White Queen (1994–2001). The title is replaced by *Generation Next* during the *Age of Apocalypse* event.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025115–118-113)
* Other Teams
  + *[X-Factor](/wiki/X-Factor_(comic_book) "X-Factor (comic book)")*, vol. 1 – the new team worked for the Pentagon replacing Freedom Force as the government-sponsored team (1991–1997); Forge later leads the mutant team as an underground government strike force (1997–1998). The title is replaced by *Factor X* during the *Age of Apocalypse* event.
  + *[Excalibur](/wiki/Excalibur_(comic_book) "Excalibur (comic book)")*, vol. 1 – the British team expanded and stays with Moira, making Muir Island their new base (1992–1998). The title is replaced by *X-Calibre* during the *Age of Apocalypse* event.

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000s")]

Sales for the comics had declined at the turn of the century, at the same time that the film series had rekindled interest in the X-Men.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022288-114) *[Ultimate X-Men](/wiki/Ultimate_X-Men "Ultimate X-Men")*, written by [Mark Millar](/wiki/Mark_Millar "Mark Millar") and illustrated by [Andy Kubert](/wiki/Andy_Kubert "Andy Kubert") and [Adam Kubert](/wiki/Adam_Kubert "Adam Kubert") began in February 2001. The series took place in an alternate version of the Marvel Universe, co-existing with *[Ultimate Spider-Man](/wiki/Ultimate_Spider-Man "Ultimate Spider-Man")*. The reboot imagined many of the primary characters as teenagers.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022286-115)

In July of the same year, *X-Men* had its title changed to *[New X-Men](/wiki/New_X-Men_(2001_series) "New X-Men (2001 series)")* and writer [Grant Morrison](/wiki/Grant_Morrison "Grant Morrison") took over. Morrison made drastic changes, beginning with "[E Is For Extinction](/wiki/E_Is_For_Extinction "E Is For Extinction")", where a new villain, [Cassandra Nova](/wiki/Cassandra_Nova "Cassandra Nova"), destroys [Genosha](/wiki/Genosha "Genosha"), killing sixteen million mutants. Morrison also brought reformed ex-villain [Emma Frost](/wiki/Emma_Frost "Emma Frost") into the primary X-Men team, and opened the doors of the school by having Xavier "out" himself to the public about being a mutant. Jean Grey was again killed off.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021167-116) The bright spandex costumes that had become iconic over the previous decades were replaced by black leather street clothes reminiscent of the uniforms of the *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(film_series) "X-Men (film series)")* films. Morrison also introduced [Xorn](/wiki/Xorn "Xorn"), who would figure prominently in the climax of his run. In the same month, *X-Force* was revamped under indie comics writers [Peter Milligan](/wiki/Peter_Milligan "Peter Milligan") and [Mike Allred](/wiki/Mike_Allred "Mike Allred"); the series was absurdist, darkly comic, and critically acclaimed, but met with a mixed reaction from fans. The entire line-up of the group was replaced, with [Doop](/wiki/Doop_(character) "Doop (character)") the most notable new character. In September, the series changed its name to [X-Statix](/wiki/X-Statix "X-Statix").[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022295-117) Another series, *[Exiles](/wiki/Exiles_(comic_book) "Exiles (comic book)")*, also began in July.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022288-114) The *Cable* title also changed its name, to *Soldier X*.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022295-117)

[Chuck Austen](/wiki/Chuck_Austen "Chuck Austen") began his controversial run on *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men")*. In 2000, Claremont returned to Marvel and was put back on the primary X-Men titles during the [Revolution](/wiki/Revolution_(2000_comic_book) "Revolution (2000 comic book)") revamp. He was later removed from the titles in 2001 and created his spin-off series, *[X-Treme X-Men](/wiki/X-Treme_X-Men "X-Treme X-Men")*. Several short-lived spin-offs and miniseries started featuring several X-Men in solo series, such as *Emma Frost*, *Mystique*, *Cyclops*, *Iceman*, *[Blink](/wiki/Blink_(character) "Blink (character)")*, *[Chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(character) "Chamber (character)")*, *[Mekanix](/wiki/Mekanix "Mekanix")* (featuring Kitty Pryde), and *Nightcrawler*. Notable additions to the X-Men in the early '00s were [Emma Frost](/wiki/Emma_Frost "Emma Frost"),[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025168-118) [Danielle Moonstar](/wiki/Danielle_Moonstar "Danielle Moonstar"), [Husk](/wiki/Husk_(comics) "Husk (comics)") and [Northstar](/wiki/Northstar_(character) "Northstar (character)") while former villain [Juggernaut](/wiki/Juggernaut_(character) "Juggernaut (character)") became member of the X-Men. Notable story arcs of this era are "[E Is For Extinction](/wiki/E_Is_For_Extinction "E Is For Extinction")" (2001), "[Planet X](/wiki/Planet_X_(comics) "Planet X (comics)")" and "[Here Comes Tomorrow](/wiki/Here_Comes_Tomorrow "Here Comes Tomorrow")".

In July 2004, [Joss Whedon](/wiki/Joss_Whedon "Joss Whedon"), creator of the hit television show *[Buffy the Vampire Slayer](/wiki/Buffy_the_Vampire_Slayer "Buffy the Vampire Slayer")*, became the writer of the new title *[Astonishing X-Men](/wiki/Astonishing_X-Men#Volume_three_(2004–present) "Astonishing X-Men")*, with [John Cassaday](/wiki/John_Cassaday "John Cassaday") as artist. The series returned the characters to their traditional superhero costumes.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022304-119)

Chris Claremont moved from writing *[X-Treme X-Men](/wiki/X-Treme_X-Men "X-Treme X-Men")* to writing *Uncanny X-Men*, with Alan Davis doing the art. Chuck Austen moved from writing *Uncanny X-Men* to *New X-Men*, which returned to its old name of simply *X-Men*, with [Salvador Larroca](/wiki/Salvador_Larroca "Salvador Larroca"), who had been working with him on *Uncanny X-Men* doing the art. *X-Treme X-Men* was cancelled. *[X-Statix](/wiki/X-Statix "X-Statix")* ended in October 2004. Also, the X-Men returned to more traditional (if not slightly revised) costumes, as opposed to the black leather uniforms from the movies. *[New X-Men: Academy X](/wiki/New_X-Men_(2004_series) "New X-Men (2004 series)")* was also launched focusing on the lives of the new young mutants at the institute. This period included the resurrections of [Colossus](/wiki/Colossus_(character) "Colossus (character)") and [Psylocke](/wiki/Psylocke "Psylocke"), a new death for [Jean Grey](/wiki/Jean_Grey "Jean Grey"), who later returned temporarily in the *[X-Men: Phoenix - Endsong](/wiki/X-Men:_Phoenix_-_Endsong "X-Men: Phoenix - Endsong")*, as well as [Emma Frost](/wiki/Emma_Frost "Emma Frost") becoming the new headmistress of the institute.

August 2005 began the [House of M](/wiki/House_of_M "House of M") crossover, written by [Brian Michael Bendis](/wiki/Brian_Michael_Bendis "Brian Michael Bendis").[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022308-120) In January 2006, the outcome of the story was that Scarlet Witch manipulated reality to [depower](/wiki/Decimation_(comics) "Decimation (comics)") 98% of the mutant population.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022312-121) The institute, formerly run as a school, served as a safe haven to mutants who are still powered.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Several short-lived spin-offs and miniseries started featuring several X-Men in solo series, such as *Nightcrawler*, *Jubilee*, *Madrox*, *[X-23](/wiki/X-23 "X-23")*, [*Gambit*](/wiki/Gambit_(Marvel_Comics) "Gambit (Marvel Comics)") and [*Rogue*](/wiki/Rogue_(Marvel_Comics) "Rogue (Marvel Comics)"). [*Cable*](/wiki/Cable_(character) "Cable (character)") and *[Deadpool](/wiki/Deadpool "Deadpool")*'s books were merged into one book, *[Cable & Deadpool](/wiki/Cable_%26_Deadpool "Cable & Deadpool")*.

Notable additions to the X-Men have been [Armor](/wiki/Armor_(Marvel_Comics_character) "Armor (Marvel Comics character)"), [Pixie](/wiki/Pixie_(X-Men) "Pixie (X-Men)") and [Warpath](/wiki/Warpath_(character) "Warpath (character)"),[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025168–169-122) while former villains such as [Lady Mastermind](/wiki/Lady_Mastermind "Lady Mastermind"), [Mystique](/wiki/Mystique_(character) "Mystique (character)"), and [Sabretooth](/wiki/Sabretooth_(character) "Sabretooth (character)") became members of the X-Men. Notable story arcs of this decade are "[Gifted](/wiki/Gifted_(comics) "Gifted (comics)")" (2004), "[House of M](/wiki/House_of_M "House of M")" (2005), "[Deadly Genesis](/wiki/X-Men:_Deadly_Genesis "X-Men: Deadly Genesis")" (2005–2006),[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025160–161-123) "[Decimation](/wiki/Decimation_(comics) "Decimation (comics)")" (2006) and "[Endangered Species](/wiki/X-Men:_Endangered_Species "X-Men: Endangered Species")" (2007). The X-Men were also involved in the "[Civil War](/wiki/Civil_War_(comics) "Civil War (comics)")" and "[World War Hulk](/wiki/World_War_Hulk "World War Hulk")" storylines.

In September 2006, Storm married [Black Panther](/wiki/Black_Panther_(character) "Black Panther (character)").[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022315-124)

In 2007, the "[Messiah Complex](/wiki/X-Men:_Messiah_Complex "X-Men: Messiah Complex")" storyline saw the destruction of the Xavier Institute and the disbanding of the X-Men. It spun the new volume of *Cable*, following Cable's attempts at protecting [Hope Summers](/wiki/Hope_Summers_(character) "Hope Summers (character)").[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025163–164-125) *X-Men* was renamed into *X-Men: Legacy* which focused on Professor X, Rogue, and Gambit. Under Cyclops's leadership, the X-Men later reformed in *Uncanny X-Men* #500, with their new base located in San Francisco.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025164–165-126) In April 2008, a new X-Force series was launched, now led by Wolverine.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022323-127) Joss Whedon's run of *Astonishing X-Men* ended in July 2008.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022324-128)

In 2009, "[Messiah War](/wiki/Messiah_War "Messiah War")," written by [Craig Kyle](/wiki/Craig_Kyle "Craig Kyle") and [Chris Yost](/wiki/Chris_Yost "Chris Yost") to serve as the second part in the trilogy that began with "Messiah Complex," was released. *[Utopia](/wiki/Utopia_(comics) "Utopia (comics)")*, written by Matt Fraction, was a crossover of [Dark Avengers](/wiki/Dark_Avengers "Dark Avengers") and Uncanny X-Men that served as a part of the "[Dark Reign](/wiki/Dark_Reign_(comics) "Dark Reign (comics)")" storyline. A new *[New Mutants](/wiki/New_Mutants "New Mutants")* volume written by Zeb Wells, which featured the more prominent members of the original team reunited, was launched. Magneto joined the X-Men during the [Nation X](/wiki/Nation_X "Nation X") storyline to the dismay of other members of the X-Men, such as Beast, who left the team.[[129]](#cite_note-129) Magneto began to work with Namor to transform Utopia into a homeland for both mutants and Atlanteans.[[130]](#cite_note-130) After the conclusion of *Utopia*, [Rogue](/wiki/Rogue_(Marvel_Comics) "Rogue (Marvel Comics)") became the main character of *X-Men: Legacy*.

Several short-lived miniseries started featuring several X-Men in solo series, such as *Daken*, *Cable*, *Psylocke*, *Namor: The First Mutant* and *X-23*. Notable additions to the X-Men have been [Pixie](/wiki/Pixie_(X-Men) "Pixie (X-Men)"), [Karma](/wiki/Karma_(character) "Karma (character)"), [Sunspot](/wiki/Sunspot_(Marvel_Comics) "Sunspot (Marvel Comics)"), [Magma](/wiki/Magma_(character) "Magma (character)"), [Magik](/wiki/Magik "Magik"), [Namor](/wiki/Namor "Namor"), [Domino](/wiki/Domino_(character) "Domino (character)"), [Boom Boom](/wiki/Tabitha_Smith "Tabitha Smith"), [Fantomex](/wiki/Fantomex "Fantomex") and [X-23](/wiki/X-23 "X-23"). Other notable story arcs of the late '00s are "[Divided We Stand](/wiki/X-Men:_Divided_We_Stand "X-Men: Divided We Stand")" (2008), "[Manifest Destiny](/wiki/X-Men:_Manifest_Destiny "X-Men: Manifest Destiny")" (2008–2009), "[X-Infernus](/wiki/X-Infernus "X-Infernus")", "[Utopia](/wiki/Utopia_(comics) "Utopia (comics)")" (2009), "[Nation X](/wiki/Nation_X "Nation X")" (2009–2010), "[Necrosha](/wiki/Necrosha "Necrosha")" (2009), "[Curse of the Mutants](/wiki/Curse_of_the_Mutants "Curse of the Mutants")" (2010–2011), and "[Age of X](/wiki/Age_of_X "Age of X")" (2011). The X-Men were also involved in the "[Secret Invasion](/wiki/Secret_Invasion "Secret Invasion")", "[War of Kings](/wiki/War_of_Kings "War of Kings")", "[Siege](/wiki/Siege_(comics) "Siege (comics)")", "[Chaos War](/wiki/Chaos_War "Chaos War")" and "[Fear Itself](/wiki/Fear_Itself_(comics) "Fear Itself (comics)")" storylines.

* X-Men
  + *[New X-Men](/wiki/New_X-Men_(2001_series) "New X-Men (2001 series)"),* vol. 1 (flagship) – The X-Men took in dozens of students expanding the school from a training center to a legitimate school (2001–2004).
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men"),* vol. 1 – Nightcrawler and Angel co-lead the X-Men's primary field team to face new threat (2001–2004).
  + *[X-Treme X-Men](/wiki/X-Treme_X-Men "X-Treme X-Men"),* vol. 1 – Storm formed a globe-trotting team to hunt down missing copies of the Destiny's Diaries (2001–2004).
  + *[Astonishing X-Men](/wiki/Astonishing_X-Men "Astonishing X-Men"),* vol. 3 (flagship) – Cyclops leads the team of X-Men and they start presenting themselves as superheroes again.
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men"),* vol. 1 – Storm and her team continued operating as officially sanctioned mutant law enforcers (2004–2006); post *Deadly Genesis*, it featured Xavier taking a team to space to hunt Vulcan when he seeks [vengeance on the Shi'ar empire](/wiki/Vulcan_(Marvel_Comics)#The_Rise_and_Fall_of_the_Shi'ar_Empire "Vulcan (Marvel Comics)") (2006–2007); the team returned to Earth to fight a group of rogue Morlocks (2007).
  + *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_Legacy "X-Men Legacy"),* vol. 2 – Havok led a new field team consisting of Polaris, Iceman, Rogue, Gambit, Wolverine and Juggernaut (2004–2006); later featured Rogue assembling a rapid response team featuring the most dangerous X-Men former villains (2006–2007).
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men"),* vol. 1 (flagship) – The X-Men open their new base in San Francisco and invite the world's mutant to join them (2008–2009); Cyclops later decided to move the mutant population to Utopia and off U.S. soil to avoid further persecution by the government (2009–2011).
  + *[X-Men Legacy](/wiki/X-Men_Legacy "X-Men Legacy"),* vol. 1 – featured Professor X's road to recovery as well as the encounters he faced during Messiah CompleX (2008–2009); later featured Rogue as mentor to the younger mutants under the protection of the X-Men on Utopia (2009–2011).
  + *[New Mutants](/wiki/The_New_Mutants_(comic_book) "The New Mutants (comic book)")*, vol. 3 – the original team is reunited to form a new field team for the X-Men.
  + *[Astonishing X-Men](/wiki/Astonishing_X-Men "Astonishing X-Men"),* vol. 3 – the X-Men serve as protectors of San Francisco City.
  + *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(comic_book) "X-Men (comic book)")*, vol. 3 – featured team-ups between characters of X-Men and other superheroes such as Blade, Spider-Man, Ghost Rider and the Future Foundation.
* X-Men in Training
  + *[New Mutants](/wiki/The_New_Mutants_(comic_book) "The New Mutants (comic book)"),* vol. 2 – features a new group of teenage mutants attending the Xavier Institute.
  + *New X-Men: Academy X* – the school is rebuilt after Xorn's attack and Emma Frost and Cyclops are named headmasters, organizing the student body into different squads who train together.
    - *[New X-Men](/wiki/New_X-Men_(2004_series) "New X-Men (2004 series)")*, vol. 2 – After *House of M* and *Decimation*, Emma Frost disbanded all former training squads and integrated those students she deemed capable of combat to a new team.
  + *[Young X-Men](/wiki/Young_X-Men "Young X-Men")* – a group of young mutants tricked by Donald Pierce disguised as Cyclops.
  + *[Generation Hope](/wiki/Hope_Summers_(character) "Hope Summers (character)")* – Hope leads a new team, consisting of five new mutants ("five lights") that appeared on Cerebro after she manifested her powers.
* Other Teams
  + *[Exiles](/wiki/Exiles_(comic_book) "Exiles (comic book)")*, vol. 1 – a revolving team roster from different realities, which have been removed from time and space, employed by the [Timebroker](/wiki/Timebroker "Timebroker") to fix broken realities. Later the team learned the true nature of the Timebroker and later traveled through different realities to chase Proteus.
  + *[X-Statix](/wiki/X-Statix "X-Statix") –* featured a group of young mutants marketed to be media superstars.
  + *[NYX](/wiki/NYX_(2003_series) "NYX (2003 series)")* – featured a group of teenage mutants as they attempt to survive on the streets of New York City.
  + *Weapon X,* vol. 2 – featured The Underground, a group assembled by Cable to oppose the activities of the third installment of the Weapon X Project. Later featuring Wolverine, Fantomex and Agent Zero quest to find the recently revived John Sublime.
  + *[Excalibur](/wiki/Excalibur_(comic_book) "Excalibur (comic book)")*, vol. 3 – Professor X and Magneto formed a team to rebuild the devastated mutant nation of Genosha.
  + *[X-Factor](/wiki/X-Factor_(comic_book) "X-Factor (comic book)")*, vol. 3 – a mutant detective agency founded by Madrox based on Mutant Town.
  + *New Excalibur* – After *Decimation*, Captain Britain brings together a new team of Excalibur as the British government decided to become more pro-active with metahuman affairs.
  + *[District X](/wiki/District_X "District X") –* Bishop is assigned to the Mutant Town to investigate rising crime rates.
  + *[X-Force](/wiki/X-Force_(comic_book) "X-Force (comic book)")*, vol. 2 – Cable re-assembles the team to stop an immortal creature called Skornn.
  + *[X-Force](/wiki/X-Force_(comic_book) "X-Force (comic book)"),* vol. 3 – Wolverine leads a more militaristic black-ops branch of the X-Men.
  + *[X-Factor](/wiki/X-Factor_(comic_book) "X-Factor (comic book)")*, vol. 3 – the agency briefly moved to Detroit, Michigan and expanded to include several new partners.
  + *Exiles*, vol. 2 – a new team of heroes are brought together by [Morph](/wiki/Changeling_(Marvel_Comics_character) "Changeling (Marvel Comics character)"), acting as the new Timebroker.

### Early 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Early 2010s")]

In 2010, "[Second Coming](/wiki/X-Men:_Second_Coming "X-Men: Second Coming")" concluded the plot threads on *Messiah Complex* and *Messiah War*.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025173-131) Nightcrawler died in *X-Force* #26 (June 2010).[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning2022337-132) In 2011, the aftermath of the "[X-Men: Schism](/wiki/X-Men:_Schism "X-Men: Schism")" storyline led to the fallout between Wolverine and Cyclops.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025176-133) During the "[Regenesis](/wiki/X-Men:_Regenesis "X-Men: Regenesis")" storyline, Wolverine's team was featured in a new flagship series titled *[Wolverine and the X-Men](/wiki/Wolverine_and_the_X-Men_(comics) "Wolverine and the X-Men (comics)")*, Wolverine rebuilt the original X-Mansion and named it the [Jean Grey School for Higher Learning](/wiki/X-Mansion "X-Mansion"). Meanwhile, Uncanny X-Men relaunched for the first time ever and served as the flagship title for Cyclops' Team. In 2012 "[Avengers vs. X-Men](/wiki/Avengers_vs._X-Men "Avengers vs. X-Men")" served as a closure to the "House of M" and "Decimation" storylines. It featured the death of Professor X and the reappearance of new mutants after the return of the Phoenix Force.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025178–183-134) Several short-lived miniseries started featuring several X-Men in solo series, such as *Storm*, *Gambit* and *Magneto: Not a Hero* (featuring Magneto and Joseph). Notable additions to the X-Men were Warbird and [Blink](/wiki/Blink_(character) "Blink (character)").

In 2012, as part of the [Marvel NOW!](/wiki/Marvel_NOW! "Marvel NOW!") relaunch, all X-Men titles (except *Astonishing X-Men* & *Wolverine and the X-Men*) were canceled, including *Uncanny X-Men*, *X-Men: Legacy*, *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(comic_book_series) "X-Men (comic book series)")* and *[New Mutants](/wiki/New_Mutants "New Mutants")*. New flagship title *[All-New X-Men](/wiki/All-New_X-Men "All-New X-Men")* was launched which featured the original five X-Men members who were brought to the present day by Beast and were made a separate team led eventually by Kitty.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025183–186-135) The relaunched *Uncanny X-Men* featured Cyclops, his team and the new mutants, taking up residency in the [Weapon X](/wiki/Weapon_X "Weapon X") facility, which they have rebuilt into a school — the New Charles Xavier School for Mutants. An all female book titled simply *X-Men* was also launched. During [All-New Marvel Now!](/wiki/Marvel_NOW!#All-New_Marvel_Now! "Marvel NOW!"), *Astonishing X-Men* was cancelled and in its place another flagship title *[Amazing X-Men](/wiki/Amazing_X-Men "Amazing X-Men")* was launched which featured the return of Nightcrawler and became the flagship title of Wolverine's team. Also, *Wolverine and the X-Men* was relaunched and turned into mutants-in-training book. In 2013, for the 50th anniversary of the X-Men, "[Battle of the Atom](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atom "Battle of the Atom")" was published which involved members of both X-Men schools trying to decide what to do about the time-displaced original X-Men, culminating in a confrontation with a version of the Brotherhood and the X-Men from an unspecified future date.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025185–186-136) In 2014, Wolverine was killed off in the "[Death of Wolverine](/wiki/Death_of_Wolverine "Death of Wolverine")" story arc, as the conclusion of a storyline that saw him lose his healing factor after he was infected by an intelligent virus.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownfield2025186-137)

Several short-lived miniseries started featuring several X-Men in solo series, such as *X-Men Legacy (*featuring Legion*)*, *Cyclops, Magneto, Nightcrawler, Storm* and *All-New* *Doop.*

Notable additions to the X-Men have been [Firestar](/wiki/Firestar_(Marvel_Comics) "Firestar (Marvel Comics)"), [M](/wiki/M_(Marvel_Comics) "M (Marvel Comics)") and [ForgetMeNot](/wiki/ForgetMeNot_(Xabi) "ForgetMeNot (Xabi)"). Notable story arcs of this era are "[Battle of the Atom](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atom "Battle of the Atom")" (2013), "[X-Termination](/wiki/Age_of_Apocalypse#"X-Termination" "Age of Apocalypse")" (2013), "[Death of Wolverine](/wiki/Death_of_Wolverine "Death of Wolverine")" (2014), "[AXIS](/wiki/AXIS_(comics) "AXIS (comics)")" (2014) and "[The Black Vortex](/wiki/The_Black_Vortex "The Black Vortex")" (2015).

* Cyclops' X-Men
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men")*, vol. 2 – the flagship of the Cyclops' team with the Extinction Team dealing with potential threats to mutantkind's survival.
  + *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(comic_book) "X-Men (comic book)")*, vol. 3 *–* Storm's field team operating from an aeroplane to neutralize threats before reaching crisis levels.
  + *[New Mutants](/wiki/The_New_Mutants_(comic_book) "The New Mutants (comic book)")*, vol. 3 *–* Cyclops tasked the New Mutants as a clean-up team to resolve loose ends.
  + *[Generation Hope](/wiki/Generation_Hope_(comics) "Generation Hope (comics)")* – a rapid response team locating new lights as they manifested with Rogue and later Shadowcat serving as liaison
  + *[All-New X-Men](/wiki/All-New_X-Men "All-New X-Men"),* vol. 1 – the flagship of the X-titles with the original X-Men brought from the past to the present to confront their future counterparts.
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men")*, vol. 3 – the flagship of the Cyclops' team with Cyclops and the remnants of his Extinction team taking up a revolutionary course to promote mutant rights.
* Wolverine's X-Men
  + *[Amazing X-Men](/wiki/Amazing_X-Men "Amazing X-Men"),* vol. 2 – the flagship of the Wolverine's team featuring a field team with initial mission to search for the deceased Nightcrawler.
  + *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(comic_book) "X-Men (comic book)")*, vol. 4 – an all-female team dealing with new threats from Arkea and a new Sisterhood.
* Wolverine's X-Men
  + *[Wolverine and the X-Men](/wiki/Wolverine_and_the_X-Men_(comics) "Wolverine and the X-Men (comics)"),* vol. 1 *–* the flagship of the Wolverine's team featuring the faculty and student of the Jean Grey School for Higher Learning.
  + *[X-Men Legacy](/wiki/X-Men_Legacy "X-Men Legacy"),* vol. 1 *–* Rogue leads a team acting as the school's security detail
  + *[Astonishing X-Men](/wiki/Astonishing_X-Men "Astonishing X-Men"),* vol. 3 *–* Wolverine's field team forming after the attack of the Marauders.
* X-Men in Training
  + *Wolverine and the X-Men* (also served as the flagship title)
  + *[Wolverine and the X-Men](/wiki/Wolverine_and_the_X-Men_(comics) "Wolverine and the X-Men (comics)"),* vol. 2 – the summer term on the Jean Grey School that focused on Logan's legacy on his students Quentin Quire, [Evan Sabahnur](/wiki/Evan_Sabahnur "Evan Sabahnur"), and [Idie Okonkwo](/wiki/Oya_(comics) "Oya (comics)").
  + *[Spider-Man and the X-Men](/wiki/Spider-Man_and_the_X-Men "Spider-Man and the X-Men") –* Spider-Man leads a Special Class to investigate the students as requested by Wolverine before his demise.
* Other Teams
  + *[Uncanny X-Force](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Force "Uncanny X-Force"),* vol. 1 – a black ops team led by Wolverine with members from the previous strike force.
  + *[X-Factor](/wiki/X-Factor_(comic_book) "X-Factor (comic book)")*, vol. 3 – Havok stepped back in to co-lead with Polaris after the disappearance of Madrox.
  + *[X-Treme X-Men](/wiki/X-Treme_X-Men "X-Treme X-Men")*, vol. 2 – a group of heroes from alternate dimensions led by Dazzler to defeat the Ten Evil Xaviers.
  + *[X-Club](/wiki/List_of_X-Men_members#X-Club "List of X-Men members")* – the X-Men's Science Team dealing with the mutant birth crisis and the effects of [M-Day](/wiki/Decimation_(comics) "Decimation (comics)").
  + *Age of Apocalypse* – featured the X-Terminated, human resistance fighters banded together to save the human race by taking out Weapon X and his new mutant regime.
  + *[Cable and X-Force](/wiki/Cable_and_X-Force "Cable and X-Force")* – a fugitive team led by Cable to face the threats that he saw in his visions.
  + *[Uncanny X-Force](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Force "Uncanny X-Force")*, vol. 2 – a proactive team of misfit X-Men led by Psylocke.
  + *[All-New X-Factor](/wiki/All-New_X-Factor "All-New X-Factor")* – a corporate-sponsored X-Factor team hired by Serval Industries.
  + *[X-Force](/wiki/X-Force_(comic_book) "X-Force (comic book)")*, vol. 4 – the remnants of Cable's X-Force and Psylocke's X-Force merge to form a superhuman black ops.
  + *Wolverines* – a group of mutants with healing factor are captured by the Paradise escapees to help them find the adamantium-petrified body of Wolverine.

### 2015-2016

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2015-2016")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4d/Extraordinary_X-Men_17_Variant_cover.jpg/250px-Extraordinary_X-Men_17_Variant_cover.jpg)](/wiki/File:Extraordinary_X-Men_17_Variant_cover.jpg)

Variant cover of *[Extraordinary X-Men](/wiki/Extraordinary_X-Men "Extraordinary X-Men")* #17, Dec. 2016 (flagship series of this era) during the "[Inhumans vs. X-Men](/wiki/Inhumans_vs._X-Men "Inhumans vs. X-Men")" story arc. Art by Jorge Molina.

In 2015, as part of "[All-New, All-Different Marvel](/wiki/All-New,_All-Different_Marvel "All-New, All-Different Marvel")", three team books were launched: the second volume of *All-New X-Men*, the fourth volume of *Uncanny X-Men* and *[Extraordinary X-Men](/wiki/Extraordinary_X-Men "Extraordinary X-Men")*.[[138]](#cite_note-138) X-23 took on the mantle of Wolverine and got a new solo series and [Old Man Logan](/wiki/Old_Man_Logan "Old Man Logan") also received a new ongoing series when the character found himself in (from his perspective) an alternate past. During this period, the mutants dealt with the threat of the [Terrigen](/wiki/Terrigen_Mist "Terrigen Mist") cloud that circulated the world and appeared to be toxic to them, placing the X-Men at odds with the [Inhumans](/wiki/Inhumans "Inhumans"). The X-Men also dealt with Apocalypse resurfacing, and the truth of what happened between Cyclops and the Inhumans that led to his death. Storm's team resided in [Limbo](/wiki/Limbo_(Marvel_Comics) "Limbo (Marvel Comics)") and worked to bring mutants to safety away from the Terrigen. Magneto's team took on a more militant approach. Beast worked alongside the Inhumans to attempt to find a way to alter the state of the Terrigen, but later discovered that it could not be altered and would have rendered Earth toxic for mutants. This revelation caused the X-Men to declare war against the Inhumans, but this conflict ended when the Inhumans learned what was happening, with Medusa sacrificing the Terrigen cloud to save the mutants.

Notable additions to the X-Men have been [Old Man Logan](/wiki/Old_Man_Logan#Earth-21923_version "Old Man Logan") and [Cerebra](/wiki/Cerebro "Cerebro"). Notable story arcs of this era are "Apocalypse Wars" (2016), "[Death of X](/wiki/Death_of_X "Death of X")" (2016) and "[Inhumans vs. X-Men](/wiki/Inhumans_vs._X-Men "Inhumans vs. X-Men")" (2016–2017). The X-Men were also involved in the "[Civil War II](/wiki/Civil_War_II "Civil War II")" and "[Monsters Unleashed](/wiki/Monsters_Unleashed_(comics) "Monsters Unleashed (comics)")" storylines.

* X-Men
  + *[Extraordinary X-Men](/wiki/Extraordinary_X-Men "Extraordinary X-Men")* – the flagship of the X-titles with X-Men relocated to Limbo following the release of the Terrigen Mists.
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men")*, vol. 4 – Magneto leads a team of deadly mutants to deal with threats to mutantkind.
  + *[All-New X-Men](/wiki/All-New_X-Men "All-New X-Men")*, vol. 2 – the time-displaced original X-Men embarking on a roadtrip in an attempt to live normal lives.

### ResurrXion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: ResurrXion")]

In 2017, the *[ResurrXion](/wiki/ResurrXion "ResurrXion")* lineup was launched with *X-Men: Prime*. It introduced new titles; *[X-Men Blue](/wiki/X-Men_Blue "X-Men Blue")*, *[X-Men Gold](/wiki/X-Men_Gold "X-Men Gold")*, *[Weapon X](/wiki/Weapon_X "Weapon X")*, new volumes of *[Astonishing X-Men](/wiki/Astonishing_X-Men "Astonishing X-Men")* and *[Generation X](/wiki/Generation_X_(comic_book) "Generation X (comic book)"),* new solo series for *Cable*, *Jean Grey*, and *Iceman*, and continuation of the *Old Man Logan* and *All-New Wolverine* run. With the Terrigen gone, the X-Men vacated Limbo and moved to [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park,_New_York "Central Park, New York") where they returned to their heroic roots instead of constantly living in fear for their survival. Other notable changes include Kitty Pryde as the new leader of the X-Men, the time-displaced X-Men working with Magneto, Old Man Logan turning Weapon X into a black ops team, and mutant characters crossing over from [Earth-1610](/wiki/Ultimate_Marvel "Ultimate Marvel") to the Earth-616 universe. Early 2018 saw the Phoenix Force returning to earth and mysteriously resurrecting the original Jean Grey. A new series featuring the original Jean leading a team of X-Men called *[X-Men Red](/wiki/X-Men_Red "X-Men Red")* was released later that same month. Rogue and Gambit's relationship became a focal point during the *[Rogue & Gambit](/wiki/Rogue_%26_Gambit "Rogue & Gambit")* miniseries and again in the *Till Death Do Us Part* story arc in *X-Men Gold*, which saw the two finally tie the knot, and once more during the *Mr. & Mrs. X* miniseries, which saw the new couple attempt to take their honeymoon but end up involved in an intergalactic conspiracy. Other noteworthy plot points included Wolverine's return coinciding with the arrival of a mysterious new villain named Persephone, Psylocke's return to her original body, Magneto's steady return to villainy, and the time-displaced X-Men facing the consequences of their presence in the 616 timeline, and the return of Cyclops. 2019 saw a new volume of *Uncanny X-Men* released beginning with a 10-part weekly story arc. Several solo series were launched, including *Legion*, *Multiple Man, Domino, Shatterstar* and *X-23*, before the revamp of the entire *X-Men* lineup.

Mainly Charles Xavier, Cyclops, Wolverine and Jean were resurrected and time-displaced X-Men returned to their original timeline during the *Extermination* event.

Notable additions to the X-Men have been [Pyro](/wiki/Pyro_(Marvel_Comics)#Simon_Lasker "Pyro (Marvel Comics)"), [Gentle](/wiki/Gentle_(character) "Gentle (character)"), Scout, Trinary, [Wolfsbane](/wiki/Wolfsbane_(character) "Wolfsbane (character)") and [Multiple Man](/wiki/Jamie_Madrox "Jamie Madrox"). Notable story arcs of this decade are "Weapons of Mutant Destruction" (2017), "Phoenix Resurrection: The Return of Jean Grey" (2017–2018), "Poison X" (2018), "Extermination" (2018), "[Hunt for Wolverine](/wiki/Hunt_for_Wolverine "Hunt for Wolverine")" (2018), "[Return of Wolverine](/wiki/Return_of_Wolverine "Return of Wolverine")" (2018–2019), "X-Men: Disassembled" (2018–2019) and "[Age of X-Man](/wiki/Age_of_X-Man "Age of X-Man")" (2019). The X-Men were also involved in the "Venomized", "[Secret Empire](/wiki/Secret_Empire_(2017_comic) "Secret Empire (2017 comic)")" and "[War of the Realms](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Realms "The War of the Realms")" storylines.

* X-Men
  + *[X-Men Gold](/wiki/X-Men_Gold "X-Men Gold")* – the flagship of the X-titles with Kitty Pryde leading the X-Men at the new location at Central Park; an ad hoc team is led by Iceman while Kitty Pryde and her team are in prison.
  + *[X-Men Blue](/wiki/X-Men_Blue "X-Men Blue") –* the time-displaced original X-Men mentored by Magneto; an ad hoc team is led by Polaris while the Original Five is lost in space.
  + *[X-Men Red](/wiki/X-Men_Red "X-Men Red"),* vol. 1 *–* the resurrected Jean Grey leading a team to have the mutants recognized as a nation.
  + *[Astonishing X-Men](/wiki/Astonishing_X-Men "Astonishing X-Men")*, vol 4 – an ad hoc team of X-Men members reunited in London to fight the Shadow King; later featured a ragtag team of X-Men co-led by Havok and Beast against the threat of the Reavers.
  + *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men")*, vol. 5 – Jean Grey leads the remnants of the Gold and Red team after Nate Grey resurfaces proclaiming himself the mutant messiah and reshaping the world in his own view; Cyclops and Wolverine reformed the team following the dissolution of the X-Men after fighting X-Man.
* X-Men in Training
  + *[Generation X](/wiki/Generation_X_(comic_book) "Generation X (comic book)")*, vol. 2 – Jubilee's group of students at the rechristened Xavier Institute.
* Other Teams
  + *[Weapon X](/wiki/Weapon_X "Weapon X")*, vol. 3 – a black ops team of heroes and villains working together to take down a new Weapon X program.
  + *New Mutants: Dead Soul* – a team of former New Mutants and X-Factor members founded by Karma to investigate paranormal occurring.
  + *[X-Force](/wiki/X-Force_(comic_book) "X-Force (comic book)")*, vol. 5 – original X-Force members Domino, Shatterstar, Cannonball, and Warpath are on the hunt for Kid Cable.
  + *[Exiles](/wiki/Exiles_(comic_book) "Exiles (comic book)")*, vol. 3 – The Unseen recruits champions from alternate universes to save the multiverse from the Time Eaters.

### Krakoan Age of X-Men

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Krakoan Age of X-Men")]

Main articles: [Krakoan Age](/wiki/Krakoan_Age "Krakoan Age"), [Dawn of X](/wiki/Dawn_of_X "Dawn of X"), [Reign of X](/wiki/Reign_of_X "Reign of X"), [Destiny of X](/wiki/Destiny_of_X "Destiny of X"), and [Fall of X](/wiki/Fall_of_X "Fall of X")

On May 14, 2019, Marvel announced they would cancel all the X-Men titles and relaunch the entire lineup. [Jonathan Hickman](/wiki/Jonathan_Hickman "Jonathan Hickman") would have full creative control; this began with two rotating bi-weekly six-issue limited series called [*House of X* and *Powers of X*](/wiki/House_of_X_and_Powers_of_X "House of X and Powers of X").[[139]](#cite_note-139) After the 12 issues are released, Hickman penned the flagship title and several new and traditional titles were released.[[140]](#cite_note-140) It was subsequently announced in July 2019 at [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") that there would be six new X-titles as part of Marvel's **[Dawn of X](/wiki/Dawn_of_X "Dawn of X")** campaign.[[141]](#cite_note-141) Following the end of the *[X of Swords](/wiki/X_of_Swords "X of Swords")* crossover, the sequel relaunch **[Reign of X](/wiki/Reign_of_X "Reign of X")** will encompass a new era in the X-titles.[[142]](#cite_note-142) **[Destiny of X](/wiki/Destiny_of_X "Destiny of X")** featured the Second [Krakoan](/wiki/Krakoa "Krakoa") Age of X-Men after the events of *Inferno* and *[X Lives of Wolverine/X Deaths of Wolverine](/wiki/X_Lives_of_Wolverine/X_Deaths_of_Wolverine "X Lives of Wolverine/X Deaths of Wolverine")*.[[143]](#cite_note-143) Mutantkind's unparalleled growth and prosperity on the island nation Krakoa was threatened during the **[Fall of X](/wiki/Fall_of_X "Fall of X")** by the human supremicist organization [Orchis](/wiki/Orchis_(comics) "Orchis (comics)").[[144]](#cite_note-144) Marvel teased the X-Men's final battle for Krakoa's future in the two interconnected titles *Fall of the House of X* and *Rise of the Powers of X*.[[145]](#cite_note-145)

Several miniseries and one-shots started featuring several X-Men in team-up and solo series such as *Giant-Size X-Men* (featuring Jean Grey, Emma Frost, Magneto, Nightcrawler, Fantomex, Storm and Thunderbird), *Wolverine*, *Cable*, *Juggernaut*, *Sabretooth*, *Rogue and Gambit*, *Astonishing Iceman*, *Children of the Vault* (featuring Cable and Bishop), *Uncanny Spider-Man* (featuring Nightcrawler), *Jean Grey*, *Ms. Marvel: The New Mutant* and *Ms. Marvel: Mutant Menace.*

Notable addition to the X-Men have been [Synch](/wiki/Synch_(character) "Synch (character)"), [Ms. Marvel (Kamala Khan)](/wiki/Kamala_Khan "Kamala Khan"), and [Rasputin IV](/wiki/Rasputin_IV "Rasputin IV"). Notable story arcs of this era are "[X of Swords](/wiki/X_of_Swords "X of Swords")" (2020), "[Hellfire Gala](/wiki/Hellfire_Gala "Hellfire Gala")" (2021), "[Trial of Magneto](/wiki/Scarlet_Witch#The_Trial_of_Magneto "Scarlet Witch")" (2021),"[Inferno](/wiki/Moira_MacTaggert#Inferno "Moira MacTaggert")" (2021), "[X Lives of Wolverine/X Deaths of Wolverine](/wiki/X_Lives_of_Wolverine/X_Deaths_of_Wolverine "X Lives of Wolverine/X Deaths of Wolverine")" (2022), "[Judgment Day](/wiki/Judgment_Day_(Marvel_Comics) "Judgment Day (Marvel Comics)")" (2022), "[Dark Web](/wiki/Dark_Web_(Marvel_Comics) "Dark Web (Marvel Comics)")" (2022–2023), "[Sins of Sinister](/wiki/Sins_of_Sinister "Sins of Sinister")" (2023) and "Weapons of Vengeance" (2023). The X-Men were also involved in the "[Empyre](/wiki/Empyre "Empyre")", "The Last Annihilation", "[King in Black](/wiki/King_in_Black "King in Black")", "[Devil's Reign](/wiki/Devil%27s_Reign "Devil's Reign")", "[Contest of Chaos](/wiki/Contest_of_Chaos "Contest of Chaos")" storylines.
