### From the Ashes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: From the Ashes")]

Main article: [X-Men: From the Ashes](/wiki/X-Men:_From_the_Ashes "X-Men: From the Ashes")

During the panel at [SXSW](/wiki/South_by_Southwest "South by Southwest") 2024, a new era of mutant mythos is announced with the X-Men scattered across the planet as they attempt to rebuild themselves following the Krakoan Age. It introduced flagship titles for *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(comic_book) "X-Men (comic book)")*, *[Uncanny X-Men](/wiki/Uncanny_X-Men "Uncanny X-Men")* and *[Exceptional X-Men](/wiki/Exceptional_X-Men "Exceptional X-Men")*, new volumes for *X-Force*, *NYX,* *X-Factor* and *Weapon X-Men*, solo series for *[Jean Grey](/wiki/Jean_Grey "Jean Grey")*, *[Wolverine](/wiki/Wolverine_(character) "Wolverine (character)")* (Logan), *[Storm](/wiki/Storm_(Marvel_Comics) "Storm (Marvel Comics)")*, *[Dazzler](/wiki/Dazzler_(Marvel_Comics) "Dazzler (Marvel Comics)")*, *[Mystique](/wiki/Mystique_(character) "Mystique (character)")*, *[Psylocke](/wiki/Psylocke "Psylocke")*, *[Wolverine](/wiki/X-23 "X-23")* (Laura Kinney) and *[Magik](/wiki/Magik "Magik"),* and a new series for *Sentinels*.[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177)[[178]](#cite_note-Marston-2024-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)

Notable addition to the X-Men have been [Kid Omega](/wiki/Quentin_Quire "Quentin Quire") and [Temper](/wiki/Oya_(comics) "Oya (comics)"). Notable story arc of this era are "Raid on Graymalkin" (2024), "X-Manhunt" (2025), "Giant-Size X-Men" (2025), and "[Age of Revelation](/wiki/Age_of_Revelation "Age of Revelation")" (2025). The X-Men were also involved in the "[Blood Hunt](/wiki/Blood_Hunt_(comics) "Blood Hunt (comics)")" and "[One World Under Doom](/wiki/One_World_Under_Doom "One World Under Doom")" storylines.

### Shadows of Tomorrow

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Shadows of Tomorrow")]

Main article: [X-Men: Shadows of Tomorrow](/wiki/X-Men:_Shadows_of_Tomorrow "X-Men: Shadows of Tomorrow")

Announced during [NYCC](/wiki/New_York_Comic_Con "New York Comic Con") 2025, the relaunch of the X-Men line of comic books will see the team united in the aftermath of *[Age of Revelation](/wiki/Age_of_Revelation "Age of Revelation")*. The announcement confirmed continuation of the series *X-Men*, *Uncanny X-Men*, and *Wolverine*, new ongoing series *Inglorious X-Force*, *Generation X-23*, *Storm: Earth's Mightiest Mutant*, and *Wade Wilson: Deadpool*, and new limited series *Cyclops*, *Rogue*, and *Magik & Colossus*.[[189]](#cite_note-189) A later announcement was made for a new ongoing series *X-Men United* featuring Emma Frost founding the Graymatter Lane and a limited series for *Moonstar*.[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191)

## Enemies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Enemies")]

Main article: [List of X-Men enemies](/wiki/List_of_X-Men_enemies "List of X-Men enemies")

The X-Men have a rogues gallery of enemies they fight on a regular basis, the most well-known being [Magneto](/wiki/Magneto_(Marvel_Comics) "Magneto (Marvel Comics)"), the mutant Master of Magnetism and Professor X's former friend.

Other popular enemies include the shapeshifting [Mystique](/wiki/Mystique_(character) "Mystique (character)"), the ancient mutant [Apocalypse](/wiki/Apocalypse_(character) "Apocalypse (character)"), the mad scientist [Mister Sinister](/wiki/Mister_Sinister "Mister Sinister"), the mutant-hunting [Sentinel](/wiki/Sentinel_(comics) "Sentinel (comics)") robots, villain teams such as the [Brotherhood of Evil Mutants](/wiki/Brotherhood_of_Evil_Mutants "Brotherhood of Evil Mutants") and the [Hellfire Club](/wiki/Hellfire_Club_(comics) "Hellfire Club (comics)"), and the constant racism and discrimination from humans.

## Themes and motifs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Themes and motifs")]

The X-Men use many recurring plot-devices and motifs for their various story arcs over the years that have become commonplace within the X-Men canon.

### Reflecting social issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Reflecting social issues")]

The conflict between mutants and normal humans is often compared to real-world conflicts experienced by minority groups in [America](/wiki/America "America") such as [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), various religious or non-religious groups, [ethnic minorities](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities "Ethnic minorities"), [autistic](/wiki/Autistic "Autistic") individuals, the [LGBTQ community](/wiki/LGBT_community "LGBT community"), etc.[[218]](#cite_note-219)[[219]](#cite_note-220) It has been remarked that attitudes towards mutants do not make sense in the context of the Marvel Universe, since non-mutants with similar powers are rarely regarded with fear; *X-Men* editor [Ann Nocenti](/wiki/Ann_Nocenti "Ann Nocenti") remarked that "I think that's literary, really – because there is no difference between Colossus and [the Torch](/wiki/Human_Torch "Human Torch"). If a guy comes into my office in flames, or a guy comes into my office and turns to steel, I'm going to have the same reaction. It doesn't really matter that I know their origins. ... as a book, *The X-Men* has always represented something different – their powers arrive at puberty, making them analogous to the changes you go through at adolescence – whether they're special, or out of control, or setting you apart – the misfit identity theme."[[220]](#cite_note-221) Also on an individual level, a number of X-Men serve a [metaphorical](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor") function as their powers illustrate points about the nature of the outsider.

> The X-Men are hated, feared and despised collectively by humanity for no other reason than that they are mutants. So what we have here, intended or not, is a book that is about racism, bigotry and prejudice.

—*Uncanny X-Men* writer [Chris Claremont](/wiki/Chris_Claremont "Chris Claremont"), 1981

* [Racism](/wiki/Racism "Racism"): Although this was not initially the case, [Professor X](/wiki/Professor_X "Professor X") has come to be compared to [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") leader [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") and [Magneto](/wiki/Magneto_(Marvel_Comics) "Magneto (Marvel Comics)") to the more militant [Malcolm X](/wiki/Malcolm_X "Malcolm X").[[221]](#cite_note-222)[[222]](#cite_note-X-Men_as_J_Men-223)[[223]](#cite_note-224) (Magneto, in the [first film](/wiki/X-Men_(film) "X-Men (film)"), quotes Malcolm X with the line "[By any means necessary](/wiki/By_any_means_necessary "By any means necessary").") X-Men comic books have often portrayed mutants as victims of [mob violence](/wiki/Riot "Riot"), evoking images of the [lynching](/wiki/Lynching "Lynching") of African Americans in the age before the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement").[[224]](#cite_note-225) Sentinels and anti-mutant hate groups such as [Friends of Humanity](/wiki/Friends_of_Humanity "Friends of Humanity"), Humanity's Last Stand, the [Church of Humanity](/wiki/Church_of_Humanity_(comics) "Church of Humanity (comics)"), and [Stryker's](/wiki/William_Stryker "William Stryker") [Purifiers](/wiki/Purifiers_(Marvel_Comics) "Purifiers (Marvel Comics)") are thought to often represent oppressive forces like the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") giving a form to denial of civil rights and amendments.[[225]](#cite_note-Prejudice_lessons_from_the_Xavier_Institute-226)

:   1980s storylines involving the fictional island nation of [Genosha](/wiki/Genosha "Genosha"), where mutants are segregated and enslaved by an [apartheid](/wiki/Crime_of_apartheid "Crime of apartheid") state, are widely interpreted as a reference to the contemporary situation of [apartheid in South Africa](/wiki/Apartheid_in_South_Africa "Apartheid in South Africa").[[226]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Comic_Books_and_Graphic_Novels-227) *[Chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(character) "Chamber (character)")* (2002) explicitly cites the [Norman Rockwell](/wiki/Norman_Rockwell "Norman Rockwell") painting *[The Problem We All Live With](/wiki/The_Problem_We_All_Live_With "The Problem We All Live With")*. The miniseries portrays using the mutant context [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_the_United_States "Affirmative action in the United States"), [National Guard](/wiki/New_York_National_Guard "New York National Guard") troops escorting a new student, sympathetic and antagonistic majority members, and majority-supremacist terrorism. Some mutants avoid confrontation and seek integration, while more militant mutants [play the race card](/wiki/Play_the_race_card "Play the race card"), reject their [human-given names](/wiki/Slave_name "Slave name"), and denounce those who do not as [Stepin Fetchit](/wiki/Stepin_Fetchit "Stepin Fetchit") and [Uncle Tom](/wiki/Uncle_Tom "Uncle Tom").[[227]](#cite_note-chamberv1no1-228)

* [Antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism"): Explicitly referenced in recent decades is the comparison between antimutant sentiment and antisemitism. Magneto, a [Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") survivor, sees the situation of mutants as similar to those of Jews in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany").[[222]](#cite_note-X-Men_as_J_Men-223)[[228]](#cite_note-X-Men_and_philosophy-229) At one point he even utters the words "never again" in a 1992 episode of the *X-Men* animated series. The mutant slave labor camps on the island of [Genosha](/wiki/Genosha "Genosha"), in which numbers were burned into mutant's foreheads, show much in common with [Nazi concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps"),[[228]](#cite_note-X-Men_and_philosophy-229)[[229]](#cite_note-230)[[230]](#cite_note-TeenStuff-231) as do the internment camps of the classic "[Days of Future Past](/wiki/Days_of_Future_Past "Days of Future Past")" storyline.[[231]](#cite_note-232) In the third X-Men film, when asked by Callisto: "If you're so proud of being a mutant, then where's your mark?" Magneto shows his concentration camp tattoo, while mentioning that he will never let another needle touch his skin. In the prequel film *[X-Men: First Class](/wiki/X-Men:_First_Class "X-Men: First Class")*, a fourteen-year-old Magneto suffers [Nazi human experimentation](/wiki/Nazi_human_experimentation "Nazi human experimentation") during his time in the camps and witnesses his mother's death by gunshot.
* [Diversity](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism"): Characters within the X-Men mythos hail from a wide variety of nationalities. These characters also reflect religious, ethnic or sexual minorities. Examples include Kitty Pryde/Shadowcat, Magneto and [Sabra](/wiki/Sabra_(character) "Sabra (character)") who are [of Jewish descent](/wiki/Jews "Jews"). [Dust](/wiki/Dust_(character) "Dust (character)"), [Ms. Marvel](/wiki/Kamala_Khan "Kamala Khan"), and [M](/wiki/M_(Marvel_Comics) "M (Marvel Comics)") who are [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"), [Nightcrawler](/wiki/Nightcrawler_(character) "Nightcrawler (character)") who is a devout [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"). [Neal Shaara/Thunderbird](/wiki/Thunderbird_(Neal_Shaara) "Thunderbird (Neal Shaara)") who is [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"). Jubilee is [Chinese American](/wiki/Chinese_American "Chinese American"), [Gambit](/wiki/Gambit_(Marvel_Comics) "Gambit (Marvel Comics)") is born to [Cajun](/wiki/Cajun "Cajun") parents from [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") and [Rogue](/wiki/Rogue_(Marvel_Comics) "Rogue (Marvel Comics)") is from Caledecott County, [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi") both of whom are [Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners"). Warpath and his brother, the Thunderbird, are [Native Americans of Apache descent](/wiki/Apache "Apache"). [Storm](/wiki/Storm_(Marvel_Comics) "Storm (Marvel Comics)") represents two aspects of the African diaspora as her father was [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") and her mother was [Kenyan](/wiki/Kenyan "Kenyan"). [Karma](/wiki/Karma_(character) "Karma (character)") was portrayed as a devout Catholic from [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), who regularly attended Mass and confession when she was introduced as a founding member of [the New Mutants](/wiki/New_Mutants "New Mutants").[[232]](#cite_note-233) This team also included [Wolfsbane](/wiki/Wolfsbane_(character) "Wolfsbane (character)") (a devout [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian")), [Danielle Moonstar](/wiki/Danielle_Moonstar "Danielle Moonstar") (a [Native American of Cheyenne descent](/wiki/Cheyenne_(ethnic_group) "Cheyenne (ethnic group)")), [Cannonball](/wiki/Cannonball_(Marvel_Comics) "Cannonball (Marvel Comics)"), and was later joined by [Magma](/wiki/Magma_(character) "Magma (character)") (a devout [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman "Greco-Roman") classical religionist). Different nationalities included Wolverine, [Aurora](/wiki/Aurora_(comics) "Aurora (comics)"), [Northstar](/wiki/Northstar_(character) "Northstar (character)"), Deadpool and [Transonic](/wiki/Transonic_(comics) "Transonic (comics)") from [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"); Colossus and Magik from [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"); Banshee and [Siryn](/wiki/Siryn "Siryn") from [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"); Dust from [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"); Psylocke, Wolfsbane and [Chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(character) "Chamber (character)") from the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"); Sunfire, [Armor](/wiki/Armor_(Marvel_Comics_character) "Armor (Marvel Comics character)"), [Surge](/wiki/Surge_(Marvel_Comics) "Surge (Marvel Comics)") and [Zero](/wiki/Kenji_Uedo "Kenji Uedo") from [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"); Sunspot from [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"); M from [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"); Nightcrawler from [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"); Sabra from [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"); [Omega Sentinel](/wiki/Karima_Shapandar "Karima Shapandar"), Neal Shaara, [Kavita Rao](/wiki/Kavita_Rao "Kavita Rao"), [Indra](/wiki/Indra_(character) "Indra (character)") and [Trinary](/wiki/Trinary_(character) "Trinary (character)") from [India](/wiki/India "India"); [Velocidad](/wiki/Velocidad_(comics) "Velocidad (comics)") from [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"); [Oya](/wiki/Oya_(comics) "Oya (comics)") from [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"); [Primal](/wiki/Primal_(Marvel_Comics) "Primal (Marvel Comics)") from [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"); etc.[[225]](#cite_note-Prejudice_lessons_from_the_Xavier_Institute-226)[[233]](#cite_note-chron-234)[[234]](#cite_note-235)
* [LGBT themes](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States"): Some commentators have noted the similarities between the struggles of mutants and the [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT_social_movements "LGBT social movements") community, noting the onset of special powers around [puberty](/wiki/Puberty "Puberty") and the parallels between being [closeted](/wiki/Closeted "Closeted") and the mutants' concealment of their powers.[[235]](#cite_note-236) In the comics series, gay and [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexuality "Bisexuality") characters include [Anole](/wiki/Anole_(character) "Anole (character)"), [Bling!](/wiki/Bling! "Bling!"), Destiny, Karma, Mystique, Psylocke, Courier, [Northstar](/wiki/Northstar_(character) "Northstar (character)") (whose marriage was depicted in the comics in 2012), [Graymalkin](/wiki/Graymalkin "Graymalkin"), [Rictor](/wiki/Rictor "Rictor"), [Shatterstar](/wiki/Shatterstar "Shatterstar"), Shade, the [Ultimate version](/wiki/Ultimate_Marvel "Ultimate Marvel") of Colossus and later Iceman after revealing that he is a mutant; the comics version of the character was then revealed to be gay in 2015. [Transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") issues also come up with shapechangers like Mystique, [Copycat](/wiki/Copycat_(Marvel_Comics) "Copycat (Marvel Comics)"), and Courier who can change gender at will.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolk2021148–149-237) It has been said that the comic books and the X-Men animated series delved into the [AIDS](/wiki/AIDS "AIDS") epidemic with a long-running plot line about the [Legacy Virus](/wiki/Legacy_Virus "Legacy Virus"), a seemingly incurable disease thought at first to attack only mutants (similar to the AIDS virus which at first was spread through the gay community).[[237]](#cite_note-238) In the film *[X-Men: First Class](/wiki/X-Men:_First_Class "X-Men: First Class"),* [Hank McCoy](/wiki/Beast_(Marvel_Comics) "Beast (Marvel Comics)") is asked by his CIA boss why he never disclosed his mutant identity, and his response was *["you didn't ask, I didn't tell"](/wiki/Don%27t_ask,_don%27t_tell "Don't ask, don't tell").*
* [Communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism"): Occasionally, undercurrents of the real-life "[Red Scare](/wiki/Red_Scare "Red Scare")" are present or the events of the Red Scare in history are alluded to. Senator Robert Kelly's proposal of a Mutant Registration Act is similar to the efforts of [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") to try to ban [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") in the United States.[[230]](#cite_note-TeenStuff-231) In the 2000 *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(film) "X-Men (film)")* film, Kelly exclaims, "We must know who these mutants are and what they can do," even brandishing a "list" of known mutants (a reference to Senator [Joseph McCarthy](/wiki/Joseph_McCarthy "Joseph McCarthy")'s list of [Communist Party USA](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA") members who were working in the government).[[238]](#cite_note-239)
* [Religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"): Religion is an integral part of several X-Men storylines. It is presented as both a positive and negative force, sometimes in the same story. The comics explore [religious fundamentalism](/wiki/Religious_fundamentalism "Religious fundamentalism") through the person of William Stryker and his Purifiers, an antimutant group that emerged in the 1982 graphic novel *[God Loves, Man Kills](/wiki/X-Men:_God_Loves,_Man_Kills "X-Men: God Loves, Man Kills").* The Purifiers believe that mutants are not human beings but children of the devil, and have attempted to exterminate them several times, most recently in the "Childhood's End" storyline. By contrast, religion is also central to the lives of several X-Men, such as Nightcrawler, a devout [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), and [Dust](/wiki/Dust_(character) "Dust (character)"), a devout [Sunni Muslim](/wiki/Sunni_Muslim "Sunni Muslim") who wears an Islamic [niqāb](/wiki/Niq%C4%81b "Niqāb").[[233]](#cite_note-chron-234)
* [Subculture](/wiki/Subculture "Subculture"): In some cases, the mutants of the X-Men universe sought to create a subculture of the typical mutant society portrayed. The Morlocks, though mutants like those attending Xavier's school, hide away from society within the tunnels of New York. These Morlock tunnels serve as the backdrop for several X-Men stories, most notably *The [Mutant Massacre](/wiki/Mutant_Massacre "Mutant Massacre")* crossover. This band of mutants illustrates another dimension to the comic, that of a group that further needs to isolate itself because society will not accept it.[[239]](#cite_note-240)[[240]](#cite_note-241) In [Grant Morrison](/wiki/Grant_Morrison "Grant Morrison")'s stories of the early 2000s, mutants are portrayed as a distinct subculture with "mutant bands," mutant use of code-names as their primary form of self-identity (rather than their given birth names), and a popular mutant fashion designer who created outfits tailored to mutant [physiology](/wiki/Physiology "Physiology"). The series *[District X](/wiki/District_X#District_X_(comic) "District X")* takes place in an area of New York City called "Mutant Town."[[226]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Comic_Books_and_Graphic_Novels-227) These instances can also serve as analogies for the way that minority groups establish subcultures and neighborhoods of their own that distinguish them from the broader general culture. Director [Bryan Singer](/wiki/Bryan_Singer "Bryan Singer") has remarked that the X-Men franchise has served as a metaphor for acceptance of all people for their special and unique gifts. The mutant condition that is often kept secret from the world can be analogous to feelings of difference and fear usually developed in everyone during adolescence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide"): Genocide and its psychological aftereffects, primarily [survivor guilt](/wiki/Survivor_guilt "Survivor guilt"), are recurring elements in some of the most significant X-Men story arcs. Magneto was a survivor of [The Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") and witnessed the genocide of his people, severely scarring him emotionally and leaving him with a strong distrust of humanity. Because of this he constantly toes the line between ally and enemy of the X-Men. The iconic [Days of Future Past](/wiki/Days_of_Future_Past "Days of Future Past") story line saw an alternate future where Sentinels committed genocide on most of the world's mutants.[[241]](#cite_note-242) In Rachel Summers' original timeline, she was captured by humans and turned into a 'hound' used to hunt down other mutants to capture and kill them, leaving her extremely traumatized by the experience and knowledge that she unwittingly assisted in the genocide of her own people. Bishop's childhood consisted of him being trapped in a mutant concentration camp, leaving him so emotionally scarred as an adult that upon returning to the past he was prepared to [kill a baby who might have caused his future](/wiki/X-Men:_Messiah_Complex "X-Men: Messiah Complex"). When [Cassandra Nova](/wiki/Cassandra_Nova "Cassandra Nova") committed genocide on Genosha, the event left both [Emma Frost](/wiki/Emma_Frost "Emma Frost")[[242]](#cite_note-243) and [Polaris](/wiki/Polaris_(Marvel_Comics) "Polaris (Marvel Comics)")[[243]](#cite_note-244)[[244]](#cite_note-245) traumatized by survivor's guilt as they were amongst the limited few survivors. While taking some time off in Germany, [Nightcrawler](/wiki/Nightcrawler_(character) "Nightcrawler (character)") witnessed the genocide of numerous mutants. The event left him as an emotional shell of who he used to be because of the trauma of what he witnessed until he had psychic therapy with [Jean](/wiki/Jean_Grey#Time-displaced_incarnation "Jean Grey") to help him cope.[[245]](#cite_note-246) Other characters who have either committed or have survived genocide include Mystique, [Callisto](/wiki/Callisto_(comics) "Callisto (comics)"), Apocalypse, Onslaught, [Bastion](/wiki/Bastion_(comics) "Bastion (comics)"), [Mister Sinister](/wiki/Mister_Sinister "Mister Sinister"), Hope Summers, Cable, and the [Phoenix Force](/wiki/Phoenix_Force "Phoenix Force").

### Time travel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Time travel")]

Many of the X-Men's stories delve into [time travel](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel") either in the sense of the team traveling through time on a mission, villains traveling through time to alter history, or certain characters traveling from the past or future to join the present team. Story arcs and spin-offs that are notable for using this plot device include *[Days of Future Past](/wiki/Days_of_Future_Past "Days of Future Past")*, *[Messiah Complex](/wiki/X-Men:_Messiah_Complex "X-Men: Messiah Complex")*, *[All-New X-Men](/wiki/All-New_X-Men "All-New X-Men")*, *[Messiah War](/wiki/Messiah_War "Messiah War")*, and *[Battle of the Atom](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atom "Battle of the Atom")*. Characters who are related to time travel include: Apocalypse, Bishop, Cable, [Old Man Logan](/wiki/Old_Man_Logan "Old Man Logan"), [Prestige](/wiki/Rachel_Summers "Rachel Summers"), Hope Summers, [Tempus](/wiki/Eva_Bell "Eva Bell"), and [Stryfe](/wiki/Stryfe "Stryfe").[[246]](#cite_note-247) A major notable period in the X-Men's history began in 2012s *[All-New X-Men](/wiki/All-New_X-Men "All-New X-Men")* when [Beast](/wiki/Beast_(Marvel_Comics) "Beast (Marvel Comics)") used time-travel to bring the original five 1960s X-Men into the present. These time-displaced characters subsequently starred in their own title *[X-Men Blue](/wiki/X-Men_Blue "X-Men Blue")* before returning to their original timeline in 2018s *Extermination* event.

### Death and resurrection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Death and resurrection")]

One of the most recurring plot devices used in the X-Men franchise is death and resurrection, mostly in the sense of Jean Grey and her bond with the Phoenix. Though not as iconic as Jean and the Phoenix, many other X-Men characters have died and come back to life on occasion. Death and resurrection has become such a common occurrence in the X-books that the characters have mentioned on numerous occasions that they are not strangers to death or have made comments that death does not always have a lasting effect on them (for example, "In mutant heaven, there are no pearly gates, only revolving doors"). [X-Necrosha](/wiki/X-Necrosha "X-Necrosha") is a particular story arc that sees [Selene](/wiki/Selene_(comics) "Selene (comics)") temporarily reanimate many of the X-Men's dead allies and enemies in order for her to achieve godhood. In the Krakoan era, the characters invent a method of resurrecting mutants who have died; becoming a significant story element across the various X-Men books.

### Fate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Fate")]

Many of the characters deal with the topic of fate. In particular, [Destiny](/wiki/Destiny_(Irene_Adler) "Destiny (Irene Adler)")'s abilities of precognition have affected certain plot points in the X-Men's history long after she was killed off due to both the X-Men and their enemies constantly searching for her missing diaries that foretell certain futures. The topic of fate takes center stage yet again in a story arc called "The Extremists" involving attacks against the [Morlocks](/wiki/Morlocks_(comics) "Morlocks (comics)") due to one of them seeing a dark future for their people.[[247]](#cite_note-248) Some characters believe they already know their own fates, such as Apocalypse believing he is fated to rule the mutants or Magneto believing he is fated to lead the mutants to rise up against humans. Other characters such as Jean, Prestige, [Evan Sabanur](/wiki/Apocalypse_(character)#Evan_Sabahnur "Apocalypse (character)"), Hope Summers, and [Warren Worthington III](/wiki/Warren_Worthington_III "Warren Worthington III") have all been wary of their fates and have all taken measures to alter their futures.

### Space travel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Space travel")]

[Space travel](/wiki/Spacefaring "Spacefaring") has been a common staple in the X-Men books beginning with the Phoenix and Dark Phoenix sagas. Since then space has been involved in many stories involving the X-Men's allies and occasional rivals the [Shi'ar](/wiki/Shi%27ar "Shi'ar") along with stories involving the Phoenix Force. Space has been the setting for many stories involving the likes of [The Brood](/wiki/Brood_(comics) "Brood (comics)"), such as the story arc where the villainous species was first introduced.[[248]](#cite_note-249) Through space noteworthy characters like The [Starjammers](/wiki/Starjammers "Starjammers") and [Vulcan](/wiki/Vulcan_(Marvel_Comics) "Vulcan (Marvel Comics)") (lost brother of Cyclops and Havok) were introduced. Space travel played a major role in [Joss Whedon](/wiki/Joss_Whedon "Joss Whedon")'s run on [Astonishing X-Men](/wiki/Astonishing_X-Men "Astonishing X-Men") via the introduction of [S.W.O.R.D.](/wiki/S.W.O.R.D. "S.W.O.R.D.") and especially in one of the final story arcs under his authorship called "Unstoppable".[[249]](#cite_note-250)[[250]](#cite_note-251) Other notable story arcs involving space included "[X-Men: The End](/wiki/X-Men:_The_End "X-Men: The End")", "Rise and Fall of the Shi'ar Empire", "[X-Men: Kingbreaker](/wiki/X-Men:_Kingbreaker "X-Men: Kingbreaker")", "[War of Kings](/wiki/War_of_Kings "War of Kings")", and "[The Black Vortex](/wiki/The_Black_Vortex "The Black Vortex")".

### Sanity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Sanity")]

The topic of sanity has been addressed in many of the major heroes and villains of X-Men. Most famously this is addressed in Jean Grey when she gains near omnipotence through the Phoenix and [Professor Xavier](/wiki/Professor_X "Professor X") after he violently uses his powers against Magneto, unintentionally creating Onslaught. Mystique's sanity wavers throughout the franchise as her constant transformations causes more and more of her mind to fracture.[[251]](#cite_note-252)[[252]](#cite_note-253) Ever since swapping bodies with [Revanche](/wiki/Kwannon_(character) "Kwannon (character)"), Psylocke has occasionally struggled to maintain her sanity due to her more aggressive nature and new powers. The character Deadpool is famous for his blatant lack of sanity. After Magneto stripped Wolverine of his metal bones, Wolverine began to become increasingly feral throughout most of the mid to late 1990s X-Men comics. The nature of Rogue's powers affecting her sanity due to her retaining the memories of others has been a central plot device on many occasions, most famously retaining [Ms. Marvel's](/wiki/Carol_Danvers "Carol Danvers") psyche throughout most of the 1980s. Most recently Emma Frost's sanity has become fractured ever since Cyclops died in her arms, causing her to declare war against Inhumans.[[253]](#cite_note-254) Other characters who have had issues with sanity include Cyclops, Sabretooth, [Magik](/wiki/Magik "Magik"), [Quentin Quire](/wiki/Quentin_Quire "Quentin Quire"), X-23, and Prestige.

### Political warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Political warfare")]

In the Marvel Universe, mutant rights is one of the hot controversial political topics and is something that is addressed numerous times in the X-books as a plot device. While some politicians like [Valerie Cooper](/wiki/Valerie_Cooper "Valerie Cooper") have legitimately tried to help the X-Men, most have made it their mission to discredit the X-Men to eliminate mutants once and for all. Senator [Robert Kelly](/wiki/Robert_Kelly_(character) "Robert Kelly (character)") began his platform on a strong outspoken anti-mutant sentiment until he changed his mind after being rescued by mutants later on in his career. When Sabretooth's human son [Graydon Creed](/wiki/Graydon_Creed "Graydon Creed") ran for office, the X-Men sent in [Cannonball](/wiki/Cannonball_(Marvel_Comics) "Cannonball (Marvel Comics)") and Iceman to discreetly join his campaign team and find anything on his anti-mutant agenda. This continued until it boiled to a head when his assassination led to "[Operation: Zero Tolerance](/wiki/Operation:_Zero_Tolerance "Operation: Zero Tolerance")." Some of the issues presented in the comics serve as allegory to modern issues in the real world, such as Lydia Nance suggesting mass mutant deportation.[[254]](#cite_note-255)

### Ideological differences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Ideological differences")]

Characters in the X-Men franchise espouse a variety of political ideologies, and these differences are a frequent catalyst for conflict. The most prominent ideological clash in the X-Men franchise is that between Xavier and Magneto; despite later interpretations of the two as analogues for Martin Luther King Jr. and Malcolm X, writer Chris Claremont (who originated Magneto's backstory and history with Xavier) saw them as more comparable to [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") and [Menachem Begin](/wiki/Menachem_Begin "Menachem Begin").[[255]](#cite_note-256) Xavier's ideology has drawn comparisons to [assimilationism](/wiki/Assimilationism "Assimilationism") and [model minority](/wiki/Model_minority "Model minority") politics, while Magneto, originally depicted as a mutant [supremacist](/wiki/Supremacist "Supremacist"), is later portrayed as a [liberationist](/wiki/Liberation_movement "Liberation movement") advocating [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") through [mutually assured destruction](/wiki/Mutually_assured_destruction "Mutually assured destruction"). [Callisto](/wiki/Callisto_(comics) "Callisto (comics)") is a [separatist](/wiki/Separatist "Separatist"), who seeks to protect the Morlocks through [isolationism](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism"). [Emma Frost](/wiki/Emma_Frost "Emma Frost") is portrayed as rejecting social movements, opting to use the [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalist "Capitalist") system for her personal benefit, or for that of individual mutants in her care. [Apocalypse](/wiki/Apocalypse_(character) "Apocalypse (character)") is characterized as a [social darwinist](/wiki/Social_darwinist "Social darwinist") who believes that mutants can only survive through the rule of might. The [Mutant Liberation Front](/wiki/Mutant_Liberation_Front "Mutant Liberation Front") commits acts of [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") to [liberate](/wiki/Liberation_movement "Liberation movement") mutants wrongly incarcerated by the government. Even when individual characters expressing conflicting ideologies are portrayed as either misguided or villainous, their motives and beliefs are often treated by the X-Men with nuance, sympathy, and respect; for example, during [Secret Wars](/wiki/Secret_Wars "Secret Wars"), when [The Avengers](/wiki/Avengers_(comics) "Avengers (comics)") take issue with Magneto's placement among the heroic team by [The Beyonder](/wiki/Beyonder "Beyonder"), the X-Men defend him as an ally, despite disagreeing with his methods.[[256]](#cite_note-257) Pulitzer-winning national security journalist [Spencer Ackerman](/wiki/Spencer_Ackerman "Spencer Ackerman") has stated on the [Cerebro podcast](/wiki/Cerebro_(podcast) "Cerebro (podcast)") that "the importance of the X-Men as a universe of stories, as a mythos, is that we should always be debating who is right."[[257]](#cite_note-Cerebro016-258)

### Media and popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Media and popular culture")]

The character of [Mojo](/wiki/Mojo_(comics) "Mojo (comics)"), an extraterrestrial being and media mogul from the [Mojoverse](/wiki/Mojoverse "Mojoverse"), serves as a satirical embodiment of the pervasive influence of media on society. Created by Nocenti and [Art Adams](/wiki/Art_Adams "Art Adams"), Mojo's grotesque appearance and obsession with television ratings are a direct commentary on the often exploitative nature of entertainment media.

Mojo's realm, where citizens are addicted to his gladiator-like television programs, mirrors the real world's fixation with reality TV and the spectacle of media. It raises questions about the ethical boundaries of entertainment and the societal impact of media consumption. The character's creation was influenced by Nocenti's readings of media critics such as [Marshall McLuhan](/wiki/Marshall_McLuhan "Marshall McLuhan"), [Noam Chomsky](/wiki/Noam_Chomsky "Noam Chomsky"), and [Walter Lippmann](/wiki/Walter_Lippmann "Walter Lippmann"), reflecting concerns about how media shapes public perception and discourse.

The X-Men's encounters with Mojo often highlight the dangers of media manipulation and the commodification of individuals. These narratives underscore the importance of critical thinking and media literacy as tools to combat the potential indoctrination by mass media. The character's storylines serve as allegories for the real-world implications of media control over information and the shaping of societal norms.

In the broader context of the X-Men universe, Mojo's machinations can be seen as a metaphor for the media's role in perpetuating social issues. His control over the Mojoverse parallels the control media conglomerates have over public opinion and the dissemination of information. The X-Men's resistance against Mojo's tyranny is symbolic of the struggle against the monopolization of media and the fight for a free and independent press.[[258]](#cite_note-SequentialTart-259)[[259]](#cite_note-260)[[260]](#cite_note-261)[[261]](#cite_note-262)[[262]](#cite_note-263)[[263]](#cite_note-264)[[264]](#cite_note-265)[[265]](#cite_note-266)[[266]](#cite_note-267)[[267]](#cite_note-268)

## Setting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Setting")]

See also: [Mutant (Marvel Comics)](/wiki/Mutant_(Marvel_Comics) "Mutant (Marvel Comics)")

The X-Men exist in the [Marvel Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Universe "Marvel Universe") along with other characters featured in Marvel Comics series and often interact with them. The X-Men/mutant corner of the Marvel Universe has been informally called "X-Universe".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] For instance, [Wolverine](/wiki/Wolverine_(character) "Wolverine (character)") was an antagonist to [the Hulk](/wiki/Hulk "Hulk") before joining the X-Men and has ties to other heroes such as [Captain America](/wiki/Captain_America "Captain America"), [Black Widow](/wiki/Black_Widow_(Marvel_Comics) "Black Widow (Marvel Comics)"), [the Thing](/wiki/Thing_(Marvel_Comics) "Thing (Marvel Comics)"), [Captain Marvel (Carol Danvers)](/wiki/Carol_Danvers "Carol Danvers"), [Elektra](/wiki/Elektra_(character) "Elektra (character)") and [Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man "Spider-Man"). [Quicksilver](/wiki/Quicksilver_(Marvel_Comics) "Quicksilver (Marvel Comics)") and [Scarlet Witch](/wiki/Scarlet_Witch "Scarlet Witch") are former [Brotherhood of Mutants](/wiki/Brotherhood_of_Mutants "Brotherhood of Mutants") members who joined the [Avengers](/wiki/Avengers_(comics) "Avengers (comics)"), as have other X-Men characters such as [Beast](/wiki/Beast_(Marvel_Comics) "Beast (Marvel Comics)"). [Rogue](/wiki/Rogue_(Marvel_Comics) "Rogue (Marvel Comics)") got her powers via absorbing Carol Danvers (then called Ms. Marvel) who has also interacted with the X-Men. [Kitty Pryde/Shadowcat](/wiki/Kitty_Pryde "Kitty Pryde") has been part of the [Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(2008_team) "Guardians of the Galaxy (2008 team)") and dated [Star-Lord](/wiki/Star-Lord "Star-Lord"), she also served as a mentor to [Franklin Richards](/wiki/Franklin_Richards_(character) "Franklin Richards (character)") the mutant son of [Mister Fantastic](/wiki/Mister_Fantastic "Mister Fantastic") and [Invisible Woman](/wiki/Invisible_Woman "Invisible Woman") of the [Fantastic Four](/wiki/Fantastic_Four "Fantastic Four"). [Storm](/wiki/Storm_(Marvel_Comics) "Storm (Marvel Comics)") was once the Queen of [Wakanda](/wiki/Wakanda "Wakanda") and the wife of [Black Panther](/wiki/Black_Panther_(character) "Black Panther (character)"), as well as a temporary member of the Fantastic Four. [Rachel Summers](/wiki/Rachel_Summers "Rachel Summers") was the girlfriend of Franklin Richards. [Iceman](/wiki/Iceman_(Marvel_Comics) "Iceman (Marvel Comics)") and [Angel](/wiki/Warren_Worthington_III "Warren Worthington III") have also joined [the original Champions](/wiki/Champions_(1975_team) "Champions (1975 team)") alongside Black Widow, [Ghost Rider](/wiki/Ghost_Rider "Ghost Rider") and [Hercules](/wiki/Hercules_(Marvel_Comics) "Hercules (Marvel Comics)") as well as having frequent partnerships with [Firestar](/wiki/Firestar_(Marvel_Comics) "Firestar (Marvel Comics)") and Spider-Man as "The Amazing Friends". [Sabretooth](/wiki/Sabretooth_(character) "Sabretooth (character)") was an [Iron Fist](/wiki/Iron_Fist_(character) "Iron Fist (character)") villain before becoming Wolverine's archenemesis. After [a conflict over the Phoenix Force](/wiki/Avengers_vs_X-Men "Avengers vs X-Men"), Captain America admitted to a remorseful but unrepentant Cyclops that the Avengers should have done more to help mutants, and not allowed the world to hate them. As a result, he formed a team called [Uncanny Avengers](/wiki/Uncanny_Avengers "Uncanny Avengers") (aka Avengers Unity Division) of both X-Men and Avengers members in hopes of unifying mutant and humankind. The global nature of the mutant concept means the scale of stories can be highly varied. The X-Men's enemies range from mutant thieves to galactic threats.

Historically, the X-Men have been based in the [Xavier Institute of Gifted Youngsters/X-Mansion](/wiki/X-Mansion "X-Mansion") located in [Salem Center](/wiki/North_Salem,_New_York "North Salem, New York"), [Westchester County, New York](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York"), and are often portrayed as a family. The Xavier's School for Gifted Youngsters/X-Mansion is often depicted with three floors and two underground levels. To the outside world, it acted as a higher learning institute until the 2000s, when Xavier was publicly exposed as a mutant at which point it became a known mutant [boarding school](/wiki/Boarding_school "Boarding school"). Xavier funds a corporation aimed at reaching mutants worldwide, though it ceased to exist following the 2005 "Decimation" storyline. The X-Men benefit from advanced technology such as Xavier tracking down mutants with a device called [Cerebro](/wiki/Cerebro "Cerebro") which amplifies his powers; the X-Men train within the [Danger Room](/wiki/Danger_Room "Danger Room"), first depicted as a room full of weapons and booby traps, now as generating holographic simulations; and the X-Men travel in their [Blackbird jet](/wiki/Blackbird_(comics) "Blackbird (comics)").

### Fictional places

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Fictional places")]

The X-Men introduced several fictional locations which are regarded as important within the shared universe in which Marvel Comics characters exist:

* **[Asteroid M](/wiki/Asteroid_M "Asteroid M")**, an asteroid made by Magneto, a mutant utopia and training facility off of the Earth's surface.
* **Avalon**, Magneto's [space station](/wiki/Space_station "Space station") that served as the primary base for him and his [Acolytes](/wiki/Acolytes_(comics) "Acolytes (comics)") to create a mutants-only safe haven after Magneto drastically reverted to his villainous ways. Originally created by [Cable](/wiki/Cable_(character) "Cable (character)").[[268]](#cite_note-269)
* **[Genosha](/wiki/Genosha "Genosha")**, an island near Madagascar and a longtime [apartheid](/wiki/Crime_of_apartheid "Crime of apartheid") regime against mutants. The U.N. gave control to Magneto until the *[E Is for Extinction](/wiki/E_Is_for_Extinction "E Is for Extinction")* story saw Genosha destroyed via mass [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide").
* **[Krakoa](/wiki/Krakoa "Krakoa")**, a living island which is currently home to the X-Men and other mutants. It is an official country.
* **[Limbo](/wiki/Otherplace "Otherplace")**, a hellish dimension heavily populated by demons. Whoever possesses the Soulsword bears control over and can draw power from Limbo. In *[Extraordinary X-Men](/wiki/Extraordinary_X-Men "Extraordinary X-Men")*, the X-Men made a sanctuary in Limbo called *X-Haven* their home after Terrigen Mist made Earth uninhabitable for mutants.
* **[Madripoor](/wiki/Madripoor "Madripoor")**, an island in [South East Asia](/wiki/South_East_Asia "South East Asia"), near Singapore. Its location is shown to be in the southern portion of the [Strait of Malacca](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca"), south west of Singapore.
* **[Mojoverse](/wiki/Mojoverse "Mojoverse")**, an alternate dimension ruled by the tyrant [Mojo](/wiki/Mojo_(comics) "Mojo (comics)") focused on creating violent [reality entertainment](/wiki/Reality_television "Reality television") usually featuring captive mutants
* **Murderworld**, fictional twisted amusement park designed by the Marvel supervillain [Arcade](/wiki/Arcade_(Marvel_Comics) "Arcade (Marvel Comics)").
* **[Muir Island](/wiki/Muir_Island "Muir Island")**, a remote island off the coast of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). This is primarily known in the X-Men universe as the home of [Moira MacTaggert](/wiki/Moira_MacTaggert "Moira MacTaggert")'s laboratory.
* **[Mutant Town](/wiki/Mutant_Town "Mutant Town")** (also known as "**District X**"), an area in [Alphabet City, Manhattan](/wiki/Alphabet_City,_Manhattan "Alphabet City, Manhattan"), populated largely by mutants and beset by poverty and crime.
* **New Tien**, a mutant-run region on the west coast of the United States where mutants outnumber humans. It was created after [Hydra took over the United States](/wiki/Secret_Empire_(organization) "Secret Empire (organization)"). Emma Frost secretly leads New Tien by telepathically possessing New Tien's [puppet ruler](/wiki/Puppet_ruler "Puppet ruler") [Xorn](/wiki/Xorn "Xorn").[[269]](#cite_note-270)
* **[Savage Land](/wiki/Savage_Land "Savage Land")**, a preserved location in [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") which is home to a number of extinct species, most notably [dinosaurs](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur"), and strange tribes.
* **Shi'ar throneworld Chandilar**, the home world of the [Shi'ar](/wiki/Shi%27ar "Shi'ar").
* **Utopia**, Cyclops had Asteroid M raised from the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") off the coast of the San Francisco as a response to the rise of anti-mutant sentiment to form a mutant nation.

## Cultural impact and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cultural impact and legacy")]

* The insecurity and anxieties in Marvel's early 1960s comic books such as *[The Fantastic Four](/wiki/Fantastic_Four_(comic_book) "Fantastic Four (comic book)")*, *[The Amazing Spider-Man](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man "The Amazing Spider-Man")*, *[The Incredible Hulk](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(comic_book) "The Incredible Hulk (comic book)")*, and *X-Men* ushered in a new type of superhero, very different from the certain and incredibly powerful superheroes before them, and changed the public's perception of superheroes.[[270]](#cite_note-271)
* The superhero team has been described as an allegory to real-life struggles experienced by people rejected by society.[[271]](#cite_note-272)[[272]](#cite_note-273)
* Ramzi Fawaz argues that X-Men presents a feminist revision of the idea of a superhero team: "As a corollary to its critique of normative identity, *The X-Men* shifted the traditional locus of affective and political identification in mainstream superhero comics from white male heroes to powerful and racially diverse female superheroes whose emotional strength anchored mutant kinships and whose superpowers granted them unprecedented ability to reshape the material world."[[273]](#cite_note-274)
* In 2022, British-American publisher Penguin Random House released a [Penguin Classics](/wiki/Penguin_Classics "Penguin Classics") edition of the early X-Men comics as part of a line of Penguin Classics editions of various Marvel comics.[[274]](#cite_note-275)

## Other versions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Other versions")]

* *[Age of Apocalypse](/wiki/Age_of_Apocalypse "Age of Apocalypse")* – In a world where Professor X is killed by his son [David/Legion](/wiki/Legion_(Marvel_Comics) "Legion (Marvel Comics)") before he can form the X-Men, Magneto leads the X-Men in a dystopian world ruled by Apocalypse. Created and reverted via time travel.
* *[Age of X](/wiki/Age_of_X "Age of X")* – a world in which anti-mutant sentiment became even worse due to a series of events and thus led to the United States government hunting down mutants with Sentinels and leading to "The Decimation" which severely reduced the mutant population and Magneto leads the mutants who are based in Fortress X.
* *[Days of Future Past](/wiki/Days_of_Future_Past "Days of Future Past")* – [Sentinels](/wiki/Sentinel_(comics) "Sentinel (comics)") have either killed or placed into concentration camps almost all mutants. Prevented by the time-traveling Kate Pryde/Widget (the adult Kitty Pryde/Shadowcat).
* *[House of M](/wiki/House_of_M "House of M")* – Reality is altered by [Scarlet Witch](/wiki/Scarlet_Witch "Scarlet Witch"), with her father Magneto as the ruler of Genosha and in which mutants are the dominant group with humans as second-class citizens. 2005's crossover event, it concludes with a reversion to the normal Marvel Universe, albeit with most mutants [depowered](/wiki/Decimation_(comics) "Decimation (comics)").
* *[Marvel 1602](/wiki/Marvel_1602 "Marvel 1602")* – Mutants are known as the "**Witchbreed**" in this alternate reality set during the time of [The Inquisition](/wiki/Inquisition "Inquisition"). Carlos Javier creates a "school for the children of gentlefolk" to serve as a safe haven and training ground for which he calls **mutantur** (or **changing ones**) and normal humans (the *mondani*). The roster consists of the original 5 members and analogues of Sunspot, Pietro and Wanda Maximoff.[[275]](#cite_note-276)
* [Marvel 2099](/wiki/Marvel_2099 "Marvel 2099") – Set in a dystopian world with new characters looking to the original X-Men as history, becoming [X-Men 2099](/wiki/X-Men_2099 "X-Men 2099") and [X-Nation 2099](/wiki/X-Nation_2099 "X-Nation 2099").
* *[Marvel Noir](/wiki/Marvel_Noir "Marvel Noir")* – The X-Men of this reality are a group of delinquent teenagers led by Charles Xavier who believes that sociopathy is the next step in human evolution.
* *[Mutant X](/wiki/Mutant_X_(comics) "Mutant X (comics)")* – Set in a world where Scott Summers was captured along with his parents by the Shi'ar and only Alex escaped, allowing him to be the eventual leader of this Universe's X-Men ("The Six"). The Mutant X universe reimagines [Mr. Fantastic](/wiki/Mister_Fantastic "Mister Fantastic"), [Nick Fury](/wiki/Nick_Fury "Nick Fury"), and Professor X as villains and [Doctor Doom](/wiki/Doctor_Doom "Doctor Doom") and Apocalypse as heroes.
* *[MC2](/wiki/Marvel_Comics_2 "Marvel Comics 2")* – In this alternate future, [Jubilee](/wiki/Jubilee_(character) "Jubilee (character)") forms the **X-People** in response to anti-mutant sentiment.[*[volume & issue needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Members include Angry Eagle, Simian, Spanner, Torque, [Push](/wiki/Nancy_Lu_(Spider-Girl_character) "Nancy Lu (Spider-Girl character)"), [Bluestreak](/wiki/Bluestreak_(comics) "Bluestreak (comics)"), [J2](/wiki/J2_(comics) "J2 (comics)"), and [Wild Thing](/wiki/Wild_Thing_(comics) "Wild Thing (comics)").[*[volume & issue needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]
* **Time-displaced X-Men** – The time-displaced team was introduced as such in *[All-New X-Men](/wiki/All-New_X-Men "All-New X-Men")* vol. 1 #1, by [Brian Michael Bendis](/wiki/Brian_Michael_Bendis "Brian Michael Bendis") and [Stuart Immonen](/wiki/Stuart_Immonen "Stuart Immonen"), and brought to the present with time travel. They were kept as regular characters, as Bendis intended to explore their reactions to the fate of their adult selves.[[276]](#cite_note-USAToday-277) The team was the main focus of the *[Battle of the Atom](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atom "Battle of the Atom")* crossover, some months later. Bendis also used them for crossovers with the [Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy "Guardians of the Galaxy") and [Miles Morales](/wiki/Miles_Morales "Miles Morales"), that he also wrote.[[277]](#cite_note-278) This was one of the few crossovers between the [Marvel Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Universe "Marvel Universe") and the [Ultimate Marvel](/wiki/Ultimate_Marvel "Ultimate Marvel") universe; Bendis preferred to write them sparingly.[[278]](#cite_note-279) *All-New X-Men* has a vol. 2 in 2015, by [Dennis Hopeless](/wiki/Dennis_Hopeless "Dennis Hopeless") and [Mark Bagley](/wiki/Mark_Bagley "Mark Bagley").[[279]](#cite_note-280) The comic was cancelled after the end of the *[Inhumans vs. X-Men](/wiki/Inhumans_vs._X-Men "Inhumans vs. X-Men")* crossover, and the team was now published in the *[X-Men Blue](/wiki/X-Men_Blue "X-Men Blue")* comic. The teenager Jean also got a solo series after the end of *[ResurrXion](/wiki/ResurrXion "ResurrXion")*, by Hopeless and Victor Ibanez, that explored her relation with the [Phoenix Force](/wiki/Phoenix_Force "Phoenix Force").[[280]](#cite_note-281) The teenager Cyclops joins the [Champions](/wiki/Champions_(2016_team) "Champions (2016 team)"), a comic book focused on teenager heroes but unrelated to the X-Men mythos.[[281]](#cite_note-282) They guest-starred in the *[Venom](/wiki/Venom_(comic_book) "Venom (comic book)")* comic, in the "Poison-X" arc. The story took the villains from the *[Venomverse](/wiki/Venomverse "Venomverse")* arc and led to the *Venomized* crossover.[[282]](#cite_note-283) The team were featured in the *Extermination* crossover, where they went back to their original timeline.[[283]](#cite_note-284)
* *[Ruins](/wiki/Ruins_(comics) "Ruins (comics)")* – Although the actual X-Men do not appear, alternate versions of its members and villains are shown to have suffered under horrific circumstances. Charles Xavier is a tyrannical [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), Jean Grey is a [prostitute](/wiki/Prostitution "Prostitution"), Magneto and Mystique die, Wolverine suffers from poisoning from his adamantium skeleton, Emma Frost heads the Church of the Next Generation and forces children of her followers to undergo surgery, Cyclops as well as Nightcrawler and Kitty Pryde are imprisoned at a Texas jail and Sabretooth is part of a fascist cannibalistic militia based in Oklahoma alongside Bucky Barnes and Jack Monroe.
* *[Ultimate X-Men](/wiki/Ultimate_X-Men "Ultimate X-Men")* – Set in the reimagined [Ultimate Marvel](/wiki/Ultimate_Marvel "Ultimate Marvel") universe. The X-Men are younger, wear black and gold uniforms and supernatural/cosmic elements are downplayed. Additionally Colossus is gay unlike his main universe counterpart, Magneto is not a Holocaust survivor and is more villainous, mutants were created by the Super-Soldier Serum, Cable is Wolverine and Kitty Pryde/Shadowcat dated Spider-Man.
* *[Ultimate Universe](/wiki/Ultimate_Universe "Ultimate Universe")* - Set in Earth-6160, a world preceded by [alternative history](/wiki/Alternative_history "Alternative history") thanks to the interference of the [Maker](/wiki/Maker_(character) "Maker (character)"). Mutants are seen as mysterious and a fringe presence to the world at large, a scattered, suppressed and unrecognized minority without central leaders due to the absence of Magneto and Professor X. A group of young outcast students led by [Armor](/wiki/Armor_(Marvel_Comics_character) "Armor (Marvel Comics character)") forms the X-Men to combat a cult of Homo Superior covertly researching their powers and believing themselves to be superior to the human race.[[284]](#cite_note-285)[[285]](#cite_note-286)
* *[X-Men Forever](/wiki/X-Men_Forever "X-Men Forever")* – An alternate continuity diverging from *X-Men*, vol. 2 #3, continuing as though writer Chris Claremont had never left writing the series.[[286]](#cite_note-Claremont_X-Men-287)
* *[X-Men Noir](/wiki/X-Men_Noir "X-Men Noir")* – Set in the 1930s, with the X-Men as a mysterious criminal gang and the Brotherhood as a secret society of corrupt cops.
* *[X-Men: The End](/wiki/X-Men:_The_End "X-Men: The End")* – A possible ending to the X-Men's early 2005 status quo.
* *[X-Men '92](/wiki/X-Men:_The_Animated_Series "X-Men: The Animated Series")* – Follows "Secret Wars", the X-Men of the 1992 TV Series, received their own comic book series.[[287]](#cite_note-288)

## In other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X-Men&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: In other media")]

Main article: [X-Men in other media](/wiki/X-Men_in_other_media "X-Men in other media")

The X-Men team has featured in multiple forms of media including the [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") [live-action film series](/wiki/X-Men_(film_series) "X-Men (film series)"), [multiple animated shows](/wiki/X-Men_in_television "X-Men in television"), live-action shows, [multiple video games](/wiki/List_of_video_games_featuring_the_X-Men "List of video games featuring the X-Men"), numerous [novels](/wiki/Novel "Novel"), [motion comics](/wiki/Motion_comic "Motion comic"), [soundtracks](/wiki/Soundtrack "Soundtrack"), [action figures](/wiki/Action_figure "Action figure"), and [clothing](/wiki/Clothing "Clothing").
